# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Classification** is a fundamental **Computer Vision** task in which a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is trained to assign a single discrete label $\hat{y} \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}$ from a predefined set of $C$ mutually exclusive categories, given an input image tensor $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C_{in}}$ (where $H$ = height, $W$ = width, $C_{in}$ = input channels, typically **3 for RGB**).

In the context of the **PCRAL508 Lab**, this experiment mandates the implementation of **three canonical CNN architectures** — **LeNet-5**, **AlexNet**, and **ResNet** — and the systematic study of their performance across varied hyperparameter configurations on standard benchmarks such as **CIFAR-10**, **MNIST**, or **Fashion-MNIST**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 The Pizza Quality Inspector Analogy**
> Imagine you are a quality-control inspector in a pizza factory. You must sort pizzas into three classes: *Cheese*, *Veggie*, and *Pepperoni*.
> 
> - **LeNet-5** is the **novice inspector** — uses a magnifying glass (small filters) and a simple checklist. Excellent for low-resolution, grayscale images like handwritten digits.
> - **AlexNet** is the **experienced inspector** — uses a larger magnifying glass, multiple checklists stacked together, and a final decision form. Handles richer, color images with millions of parameters.
> - **ResNet** is the **master inspector with a "skip rule"** — even if a step is confusing, the master can *skip back* to a previously known good observation, ensuring no critical detail is lost. This is the **skip connection** that solves the *vanishing gradient* problem.

## 1.3 Why CNNs for Image Classification?

| Property | Traditional ANN (MLP) | CNN |
|---|---|---|
| Parameter count | **Explodes** with image size (e.g., $224 \times 224 \times 3 = 150{,}528$ inputs) | **Shared weights** — drastically fewer parameters |
| Spatial hierarchy | Treats pixels as independent | **Preserves spatial topology** via convolution |
| Translation equivariance | ❌ Fails when object shifts | ✅ Inherently equivariant |
| Computational cost | Very high | Drastically lower due to weight sharing |

## 1.4 The Three Architectures — A Glance

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **depth-vs-accuracy trade-off**: LeNet (5 layers) → AlexNet (8 layers) → ResNet (50/101/152 layers). Deeper networks *can* learn more, but only when designed with **skip connections** (ResNet) to combat **degradation**.

| Architecture | Year | Depth | Key Innovation | Typical Dataset |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LeNet-5** | 1998 | 7 layers (2 Conv + 2 Pool + 2 FC) | First practical CNN | MNIST |
| **AlexNet** | 2012 | 8 layers (5 Conv + 3 FC) | ReLU, Dropout, GPU training, Data Augmentation | ImageNet |
| **ResNet** | 2015 | 50 / 101 / 152 layers | **Residual / Skip Connections** | ImageNet, CIFAR-10 |

# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a CNN Image Classifier

The end-to-end pipeline for a CNN image classification experiment follows these structured logic steps:

1. **Data Acquisition & Pre-processing** — Load dataset (e.g., CIFAR-10), normalize pixel values from $[0, 255]$ to $[0, 1]$ (or mean-std normalization), apply one-hot encoding to labels.
2. **Train/Validation/Test Split** — Typically **70/15/15** or **80/10/10** depending on dataset size.
3. **Architecture Construction** — Stack `Conv2D → ReLU → BatchNorm → MaxPool` blocks; finalize with `GlobalAveragePooling` (or `Flatten`) and `Dense (Softmax)` classifier.
4. **Loss Function Selection** — Use **Categorical Cross-Entropy** for multi-class classification:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CCE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \cdot \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$

where $N$ = batch size, $C$ = number of classes, $y_{i,c}$ = ground-truth one-hot, $\hat{y}_{i,c}$ = predicted softmax probability.

5. **Optimizer Selection** — Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) with momentum, **Adam** ($\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$), or RMSprop.
6. **Backpropagation & Weight Update** — Gradients $\nabla_w \mathcal{L}$ computed via chain rule; weights updated as $w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_w \mathcal{L}$.
7. **Hyperparameter Experimentation** — Sweep over **learning rate** $\eta$, **batch size**, **optimizer**, **dropout rate**, **filter sizes**, and **data augmentation** settings.
8. **Performance Evaluation** — Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Confusion Matrix, Top-K Accuracy** on the held-out test set.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for PCRAL508 theory viva and written components.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convolution Output Size | $O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $I$ = input, $K$ = kernel, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride |
| 2 | Receptive Field | $RF_{l} = RF_{l-1} + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i$ | Grows with depth |
| 3 | Parameters in Conv Layer | $P_{conv} = (K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot C_{out}$ | $+1$ for bias term |
| 4 | Parameters in FC Layer | $P_{FC} = (n_{in} + 1) \cdot n_{out}$ | $+1$ for bias term |
| 5 | Softmax | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}$ | Output sums to 1 |
| 6 | Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \cdot \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Used with Softmax |
| 7 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction correct |
| 8 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| 9 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate |
| 10 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| 11 | Macro F1 | $F_1^{macro} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} F_1^{(c)}$ | Unweighted mean across classes |
| 12 | Weight Decay (L2) | $\mathcal{L}_{reg} = \mathcal{L} + \lambda \cdot \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert_2^2$ | $\lambda$ = regularization strength |
| 13 | Adam Update | $w_{t+1} = w_t - \frac{\eta \cdot \hat{m}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{v}_t} + \epsilon}$ | $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$ for stability |
| 14 | Dropout (at inference) | $w_{test} = p \cdot w_{train}$ | Scale by keep-prob $p$ |

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Architectural Innovation

- **Why LeNet-5 uses alternating Conv + Pool?** To progressively **reduce spatial dimensions** while **increasing feature abstraction** (edges → textures → parts).
- **Why AlexNet uses ReLU instead of Sigmoid/Tanh?** ReLU $\rightarrow$ $\max(0, x)$ does not saturate for positive values, eliminating the **vanishing gradient** problem and enabling 6× faster training.
- **Why ResNet uses Skip Connections?** A deep "plain" network suffers from **degradation** — accuracy saturates and then degrades. The residual block learns $\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{x}$, making it easier to learn an *identity mapping* if the optimal transformation is near-identity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> CNN image classification underpins: **autonomous vehicles** (sign recognition), **medical diagnostics** (X-ray / MRI tumor detection), **defect detection in manufacturing** (PCB / fabric inspection), **satellite imaging** (crop / flood mapping), and **content moderation** on social platforms.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Calculation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Convolutional Output Dimension

Given an input feature map of spatial size $I$, kernel size $K$, padding $P$, and stride $S$, we derive the output size $O$:

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup.**
We slide the kernel across the input. The total horizontal distance traveled is $I - K$ (you cannot start a kernel of size $K$ past position $I - K$).

**Step 2 — Add Padding.** Padding $P$ effectively *expands* the input to size $I + 2P$ (padding applied symmetrically on both sides).

**Step 3 — Effective Sliding Distance.** The kernel can now slide over a length of $(I + 2P) - K$.

**Step 4 — Account for Stride.** Each step advances by $S$ units, so the number of valid positions is:

$$\text{positions} = \frac{(I + 2P) - K}{S}$$

**Step 5 — Final Formula.** Adding 1 (counting the first position) and flooring for non-integer cases:

$$\boxed{O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1}$$

**Worked Example.** Input image $I = 32$, kernel $K = 3$, padding $P = 1$, stride $S = 1$ (typical AlexNet first layer on CIFAR-10):

$$O = \left\lfloor \frac{32 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 31 \right\rfloor + 1 = 32$$

✅ **Spatial dimensions preserved** — this is the hallmark of `padding = "same"` in modern frameworks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Parameter Count for LeNet-5 vs AlexNet vs ResNet-50

### 3.2.1 LeNet-5 (CIFAR-10 adapted)

| Layer | Output Size | Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Conv1 ($5{\times}5{\times}6$) | $28 \times 28 \times 6$ | $(5 \cdot 5 \cdot 1 + 1) \cdot 6 = 156$ |
| Pool1 ($2{\times}2$) | $14 \times 14 \times 6$ | 0 |
| Conv2 ($5{\times}5{\times}16$) | $10 \times 10 \times 16$ | $(5 \cdot 5 \cdot 6 + 1) \cdot 16 = 2{,}416$ |
| Pool2 ($2{\times}2$) | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ | 0 |
| FC1 | 120 | $(5 \cdot 5 \cdot 16 + 1) \cdot 120 = 48{,}120$ |
| FC2 | 84 | $(120 + 1) \cdot 84 = 10{,}164$ |
| FC3 (output) | 10 | $(84 + 1) \cdot 10 = 850$ |
| **Total** | | **≈ 61,706 params** |

### 3.2.2 AlexNet (CIFAR-10 adapted, original $224{\times}224$ scaled to $32{\times}32$)

| Layer | Output Size | Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Conv1 ($11{\times}11, 96, S=4$) | $55 \times 55 \times 96$ | $(11{\cdot}11{\cdot}3 + 1){\cdot}96 = 34{,}944$ |
| Conv2 ($5{\times}5, 256$) | $27 \times 27 \times 256$ | $(5{\cdot}5{\cdot}48 + 1){\cdot}256 = 307{,}456$ (split groups) |
| Conv3 ($3{\times}3, 384$) | $13 \times 13 \times 384$ | $(3{\cdot}3{\cdot}256 + 1){\cdot}384 = 885{,}120$ |
| Conv4 ($3{\times}3, 384$) | $13 \times 13 \times 384$ | $(3{\cdot}3{\cdot}384 + 1){\cdot}384 = 1{,}327{,}488$ |
| Conv5 ($3{\times}3, 256$) | $13 \times 13 \times 256$ | $(3{\cdot}3{\cdot}384 + 1){\cdot}256 = 884{,}992$ |
| FC1 | 4096 | $(6{\cdot}6{\cdot}256 + 1){\cdot}4096 \approx 37{,}752{,}832$ |
| FC2 | 4096 | $(4096 + 1){\cdot}4096 \approx 16{,}781{,}312$ |
| FC3 | 10 | $(4096 + 1){\cdot}10 = 40{,}970$ |
| **Total** | | **≈ 60 million params** |

### 3.2.3 ResNet-50

| Stage | Layers | Output Size | Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conv1 | $7{\times}7, 64, S=2$ | $112 \times 112 \times 64$ | $\approx 9.4\text{K}$ |
| Conv2_x | 3 bottleneck blocks | $56 \times 56 \times 256$ | $\approx 0.2\text{M}$ |
| Conv3_x | 4 bottleneck blocks | $28 \times 28 \times 512$ | $\approx 1.2\text{M}$ |
| Conv4_x | 6 bottleneck blocks | $14 \times 14 \times 1024$ | $\approx 7.1\text{M}$ |
| Conv5_x | 3 bottleneck blocks | $7 \times 7 \times 2048$ | $\approx 15.4\text{M}$ |
| FC | — | 1000 | $\approx 2.0\text{M}$ |
| **Total** | | | **≈ 25.6 million params** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** ResNet-50 is *deeper* (50 layers) but has **fewer parameters** than AlexNet, thanks to **bottleneck blocks** ( $1{\times}1 \to 3{\times}3 \to 1{\times}1$ convolutions) and **global average pooling** replacing the FC-heavy top of AlexNet.

## 3.3 Derivation: Residual Block Forward Pass

A residual block computes:

**Step 1 — Main Path.** Apply two (or three) conv + BN + ReLU operations to input $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}) = W_2 \cdot \sigma(W_1 \cdot \mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2$$

where $W_1$ is typically a $3{\times}3$ (or $1{\times}1$) conv weight, $\sigma = \text{ReLU}$.

**Step 2 — Identity Shortcut.** Preserve the input $\mathbf{x}$ unchanged.

**Step 3 — Summation.** Add the main-path output to the shortcut:

$$\mathbf{y} = \sigma(\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{x})$$

**Step 4 — Why this works.** If the optimal learned transformation is close to identity ($\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}) \approx \mathbf{x}$), then $\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}) \approx 0$ is *easier* to learn than $\mathcal{H}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}$ directly, because zero-mapping requires fewer non-linear decisions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hyperparameter Sensitivity

| Hyperparameter | Effect of ↑ Increase | Typical Range | Risk if Too High | Risk if Too Low |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Learning rate $\eta$ | Larger weight updates | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-1}$ | Divergence / oscillation | Stagnation / no learning |
| Batch size | Smoother gradients, faster training (GPU) | 32 – 256 | Poor generalization, memory | Noisy gradients, slow |
| Dropout rate $p$ | More regularization | 0.2 – 0.5 | Underfitting | Overfitting |
| L2 $\lambda$ | Stronger weight decay | $10^{-5}$ – $10^{-3}$ | Underfitting | Overfitting |
| Filter size $K$ | Larger receptive field per layer | $3{\times}3$ – $11{\times}11$ | Overfitting, compute | Misses global patterns |
| Network depth | More representational power | 5 – 152 | Vanishing gradient (without skip) | Underfitting |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
**Define Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). State any two advantages of CNNs over traditional fully connected neural networks for image classification tasks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a class of deep neural networks designed to process data with a known grid-like topology (e.g., images). It uses **convolutional layers** that apply learnable filters to extract local features, followed by **pooling layers** for spatial down-sampling.

**Two advantages (1½ marks each):**
1. **Parameter sharing** — the same filter is slid across the entire image, drastically reducing the number of parameters (e.g., a $3{\times}3$ filter has only 9 weights regardless of image size, whereas an FC layer would have millions).
2. **Spatial locality & translation equivariance** — CNNs preserve the 2D spatial structure of images and detect features regardless of their position.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the vanishing gradient problem? How does ResNet's skip connection help mitigate it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **vanishing gradient problem** (1 mark) occurs when gradients of the loss w.r.t. weights become extremely small as they are back-propagated through many layers, causing early layers to learn negligibly and the network to stagnate.

**How ResNet mitigates it (2 marks):** ResNet introduces **skip / shortcut connections** that add the input $\mathbf{x}$ to the output of a convolutional block: $\mathbf{y} = \mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{x}$. During backpropagation, the gradient of loss w.r.t. $\mathbf{x}$ becomes $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{y}} \cdot \left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} + 1\right)$. The **"+1" term** ensures the gradient has a non-zero additive component, preventing it from vanishing regardless of how deep the network is.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Implementation**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **LeNet-5 architecture**. State its layer-wise configuration, activation functions used, and total trainable parameter count. (7 marks)

**(b)** Implement LeNet-5 in **PyTorch/Keras** for **MNIST classification**. Show the complete code and explain the role of each layer. (7 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Q.A(a) — LeNet-5 Architecture (7 marks)

**Layer-wise Configuration (4 marks):**

| # | Layer Type | Configuration | Output Size | Activation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input | Grayscale $32 \times 32$ | $32 \times 32 \times 1$ | — |
| 2 | Conv2D | $5 \times 5$ filters, 6 channels, stride 1 | $28 \times 28 \times 6$ | $\tanh$ |
| 3 | AvgPool | $2 \times 2$, stride 2 | $14 \times 14 \times 6$ | — |
| 4 | Conv2D | $5 \times 5$ filters, 16 channels | $10 \times 10 \times 16$ | $\tanh$ |
| 5 | AvgPool | $2 \times 2$, stride 2 | $5 \times 5 \times 16$ | — |
| 6 | Flatten | — | 400 | — |
| 7 | FC | 120 units | 120 | $\tanh$ |
| 8 | FC | 84 units | 84 | $\tanh$ |
| 9 | Output FC | 10 units (Gaussian RBF in original; Softmax in modern) | 10 | Softmax |

**Parameter Count (1 mark):** ≈ **61,706 trainable parameters** (as derived in Section 3.2.1).

**Key Insights (2 marks):**
- LeNet-5 uses **alternating Conv + Pool** to progressively build a spatial hierarchy: pixel → edge → stroke → digit part → full digit.
- The original used **Gaussian RBF connections** in the output, but modern implementations use **Softmax + Cross-Entropy** for end-to-end differentiability.
- Total receptive field of the final FC layer covers the *entire* input image, enabling global pattern recognition.

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Q.A(b) — PyTorch Implementation (7 marks)

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torchvision import datasets, transforms
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# (i) Data Loading & Preprocessing (2 marks)
transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize((0.1307,), (0.3081,))   # MNIST mean & std
])
train_set = datasets.MNIST('./data', train=True,  download=True, transform=transform)
test_set  = datasets.MNIST('./data', train=False, download=True, transform=transform)
train_loader = DataLoader(train_set, batch_size=64, shuffle=True)
test_loader  = DataLoader(test_set,  batch_size=1000, shuffle=False)

# (ii) LeNet-5 Model Definition (3 marks)
class LeNet5(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1,  6, kernel_size=5, padding=2)   # 32x32 -> 32x32
        self.pool1 = nn.AvgPool2d(2, 2)                          # -> 16x16
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(6, 16, kernel_size=5)             # -> 12x12
        self.pool2 = nn.AvgPool2d(2, 2)                          # -> 6x6 (close to 5x5)
        self.fc1   = nn.Linear(16 * 5 * 5, 120)
        self.fc2   = nn.Linear(120, 84)
        self.fc3   = nn.Linear(84, 10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = F.tanh(self.conv1(x))
        x = self.pool1(x)
        x = F.tanh(self.conv2(x))
        x = self.pool2(x)
        x = torch.flatten(x, 1)
        x = F.tanh(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.tanh(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)                # logits (CrossEntropyLoss applies softmax)
        return x

# (iii) Training Loop (2 marks)
device    = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
model     = LeNet5().to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(1, 6):     # 5 epochs for demonstration
    model.train()
    for xb, yb in train_loader:
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss = criterion(model(xb), yb)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
    print(f"Epoch {epoch} — Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
```

**Role of each layer (as comments and viva explanation):**
- `Conv2d(1, 6, 5)` — extracts 6 low-level feature maps (edges, gradients).
- `AvgPool2d` — translation-invariant down-sampling; halves spatial dims.
- `Conv2d(6, 16, 5)` — extracts 16 mid-level features (corners, stroke junctions).
- `Flatten + FC` — maps spatial features to a class decision vector.
- `CrossEntropyLoss` — combines LogSoftmax + Negative Log-Likelihood in one stable function.

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Evaluation & Experimentation**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** You are given a multi-class image classification task with **10 classes** and an imbalanced test set. Explain the **confusion matrix**, **precision**, **recall**, **F1-score**, and **macro-F1**. Justify why **macro-F1 is preferred over accuracy** for imbalanced datasets. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a hyperparameter experiment comparing **LeNet-5, AlexNet, and ResNet-18** on **CIFAR-10**. Specify: (i) hyperparameters to tune, (ii) evaluation protocol, (iii) expected trends with reasoning. (7 marks)

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Q.B(a) — Evaluation Metrics (7 marks)

**Confusion Matrix (2 marks):** An $N \times N$ table where entry $M_{ij}$ = number of samples whose *true* class is $i$ but were *predicted* as class $j$. The diagonal $M_{ii}$ = correct predictions.

For 10 classes ($C = 10$):

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} & \cdots & M_{1C} \\ M_{21} & M_{22} & \cdots & M_{2C} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ M_{C1} & M_{C2} & \cdots & M_{CC} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Per-class metrics (2 marks):**
- $\text{Precision}_c = \frac{M_{cc}}{\sum_{i=1}^{C} M_{ic}}$ — *of all predicted class-$c$, how many are truly class-$c$?*
- $\text{Recall}_c = \frac{M_{cc}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} M_{cj}}$ — *of all truly class-$c$ samples, how many did we find?*
- $F_{1,c} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec}_c \cdot \text{Rec}_c}{\text{Prec}_c + \text{Rec}_c}$

**Macro-F1 (1 mark):** Unweighted mean over all $C$ classes:

$$F_1^{macro} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} F_{1,c}$$

**Why macro-F1 over accuracy for imbalance (2 marks):**
Suppose 9 of 10 classes have 100 samples each (total 900) and one class has 9,100 samples. A naive classifier predicting the *majority class* always achieves $\frac{9100}{10000} = 91\%$ **accuracy**, but its macro-F1 collapses to ≈ 0.16 because the minority class has Recall = 0. **Macro-F1 weights all classes equally**, exposing the model's failure on underrepresented categories — a critical property in medical-imaging, defect-detection, and fraud-detection applications.

---

#### 🔹 Solution to Q.B(b) — Hyperparameter Experiment Design (7 marks)

**(i) Hyperparameters to tune (2 marks):**

| Category | Hyperparameters | Search Range |
|---|---|---|
| Optimizer | Algorithm, learning rate $\eta$ | {SGD, Adam}, $\eta \in \{10^{-2}, 10^{-3}, 10^{-4}\}$ |
| Regularization | Dropout $p$, L2 $\lambda$, BatchNorm | $p \in \{0.2, 0.5\}$, $\lambda \in \{10^{-4}, 10^{-5}\}$ |
| Training | Batch size, Epochs | {32, 64, 128}, 30–100 |
| Augmentation | Flip, Crop, Cutout, Mixup | ON / OFF |
| Architecture | Filter count, depth | Per-arch |

**(ii) Evaluation Protocol (3 marks):**
1. Split CIFAR-10 → **40k train / 10k val / 10k test** (standard split).
2. For each architecture, run a **grid or random search** over the search space (≥ 5 trials per architecture).
3. For each trial, record **val accuracy, val macro-F1, training time, parameter count**.
4. Select the best hyperparameter config *per architecture* based on **val macro-F1**.
5. **Retrain** the best config on `train + val` combined, then evaluate once on the **held-out test set** to report final metrics.
6. Repeat each experiment with **3 random seeds**; report **mean ± std**.

**(iii) Expected Trends (2 marks):**

| Architecture | Expected CIFAR-10 Test Acc | Expected Time / Epoch | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| LeNet-5 | ~ 60–70% | ~ 10 s | Too shallow, ~60K params; struggles with 32×32 RGB complexity |
| AlexNet | ~ 75–85% | ~ 60 s | Larger receptive fields, 60M params, ReLU + Dropout |
| ResNet-18 | ~ 90–94% | ~ 90 s | Skip connections solve degradation, deeper features |

**Reasoning (qualitative):** As depth increases from 5 → 8 → 18 layers *with* appropriate skip connections (ResNet), representational capacity grows without suffering from gradient degradation. The **bottleneck blocks** in ResNet compress parameters efficiently via $1 \times 1$ convolutions, allowing deeper networks to remain computationally tractable.

---

## 5.3 🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize inputs** — Always normalize pixel values to $[0, 1]$ or use mean/std normalization. Without this, training diverges or saturates.
> 2. **Not using `model.eval()` during validation/test** — Disables Dropout and BatchNorm running stats updates. Otherwise metrics become noisy and misleading.
> 3. **Confusing `CrossEntropyLoss` with `BCE`** — In PyTorch, `nn.CrossEntropyLoss` *internally applies* Softmax. Do **not** apply Softmax to logits beforehand or you'll double-apply it and break gradients.
> 4. **Padding errors** — Using `padding = "same"` carelessly on stride-2 convolutions can yield off-by-one dimension errors. Always compute $O$ via the formula.
> 5. **Reporting only test accuracy** — Examiners expect **macro-F1, confusion matrix, and per-class precision/recall** for multi-class imbalanced settings.
> 6. **Not setting a random seed** — Results become irreproducible; KTU expects reproducible experiments with documented seeds.

---

## 5.4 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin!)**
> 
> ✅ **Three architectures**: LeNet-5 (shallow, MNIST), AlexNet (deep, ReLU+Dropout, ImageNet), ResNet (very deep, **skip connections solve vanishing gradient**).
> 
> ✅ **Conv output formula**: $O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ — memorize and apply.
> 
> ✅ **Loss function**: Categorical Cross-Entropy with Softmax output for multi-class.
> 
> ✅ **Metrics**: Always report **Accuracy, Macro-Precision, Macro-Recall, Macro-F1, Confusion Matrix**. Use **macro-F1** for imbalanced classes.
> 
> ✅ **Skip connection math**: $\mathbf{y} = \mathcal{F}(\mathbf{x}) + \mathbf{x}$; gradient gets a "+1" term → no vanishing.
> 
> ✅ **Hyperparameters to sweep**: learning rate, batch size, optimizer, dropout, weight decay, filter size, augmentation.
> 
> ✅ **Always** — normalize inputs, set random seed, use `model.eval()` for testing, report mean ± std over multiple seeds.
> 
> ✅ **Reproducibility mantra**: *Data → Model → Loss → Optimizer → Train → Validate → Test → Report.* Document every step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Detection: Train a CNN-based object detection model (e.g., YOLO/Faster R-CNN) on a custom dataset. Evaluate performance using standard metrics.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL508 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Object Detection: Train a CNN-based object detection model (e.g., YOLO/Faster R-CNN) on a custom dataset. Evaluate performance using standard metrics.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Detection** is a computer vision task that involves **localization** (drawing a bounding box around an object) and **classification** (assigning a class label to that object) simultaneously, typically using a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** as the feature extraction backbone.

In the context of the **PCRAL508** lab syllabus, the two industry-standard CNN-based object detection paradigms you must master are:

1. **Two-Stage Detectors** — *Faster R-CNN* (Region-based CNN). The model first generates **Region Proposals** (candidate boxes) using a **Region Proposal Network (RPN)**, and then classifies + refines each proposal in a second stage.
2. **One-Stage Detectors** — *YOLO* (You Only Look Once). A single CNN directly regresses bounding box coordinates and class probabilities in **one forward pass**, trading a small amount of accuracy for real-time speed.

**Performance evaluation** is conducted using standardized metrics defined by the **PASCAL VOC** and **COCO (Common Objects in Context)** benchmarks. The two cardinal metrics are:

- **Intersection over Union (IoU)** — measures localization accuracy (a value between 0 and 1).
- **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** — the single most important detection metric, integrating precision, recall, and IoU thresholds.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / The "Intuition"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Security Guard at a Mall:**
> Imagine you are a security guard watching 50 CCTV monitors. Your job is to spot shoplifters and tell your supervisor **WHERE** they are on the screen and **WHO** they are.
>
> - **Classification alone** = telling the supervisor "Yes, there is a shoplifter" but not saying where.
> - **Localization alone** = drawing a box on the monitor but not saying who is inside.
> - **Object Detection** = doing **BOTH** in one glance.
>
> **Faster R-CNN** is like a guard who first scans the whole mall, marks all *suspicious zones* (Region Proposals), and then inspects each zone closely before deciding. It is **slow but very accurate**.
>
> **YOLO** is like a guard who, in a single sweeping glance of all 50 monitors, simultaneously shouts out the box and the identity. It is **fast but slightly less precise**, perfect for real-time tracking.
>
> **IoU** is like measuring how much your drawn box overlaps with the *actual* shoplifter's true location — a box perfectly enclosing the thief gives IoU = 1.0.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Backbone** | The pre-trained CNN (e.g., ResNet-50, Darknet-53) that extracts feature maps. |
| **Feature Map** | A 3D tensor output by the backbone encoding spatial + semantic information. |
| **Anchor Box** | A pre-defined reference box of specific width/height at a given feature-map location. |
| **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** | A small CNN that slides over the feature map and proposes candidate object regions. |
| **RoI Pooling** | Converts variable-size proposals into a fixed-size feature map for the classifier head. |
| **Bounding Box** | The rectangle $(x_1, y_1, x_2, y_2)$ or $(x_c, y_c, w, h)$ enclosing an object. |
| **Ground Truth (GT)** | The human-annotated correct bounding box + class label. |
| **IoU Threshold** | Standard values: **0.5** (PASCAL VOC) and **0.5:0.05:0.95** (COCO mAP). |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Architectural Paradigms

### A. Faster R-CNN (Two-Stage Detector)

The architecture was introduced by **Ren et al. (NeurIPS 2015)** and consists of:

**Stage 1 — Region Proposal:**
1. Input image is passed through a **CNN backbone** (e.g., VGG-16, ResNet-101) producing a **convolutional feature map**.
2. A small **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** slides a $3 \times 3$ window over the feature map.
3. At each spatial location, $k$ **anchor boxes** (typically $k = 9$, combining 3 scales $\times$ 3 aspect ratios) are evaluated.
4. The RPN outputs two sibling layers:
   - **cls layer**: $2k$ scores (object / not-object) per anchor.
   - **reg layer**: $4k$ values (bounding-box deltas) per anchor.

**Stage 2 — Detection Head:**
5. Proposals passing an **objectness score threshold** are passed through **RoI Pooling** to obtain fixed-size ($7 \times 7$) feature maps.
6. These are flattened and fed into fully-connected layers that output:
   - **Class probabilities** ($C + 1$ classes, where +1 is the background class).
   - **Bounding-box regression offsets** (4 values per class).

### B. YOLO (One-Stage Detector)

The original **YOLOv1 (Redmon et al., CVPR 2016)** revolutionized detection by:

1. Resizing the input image to $448 \times 448$.
2. Passing it through a single CNN (originally Darknet-19, later Darknet-53 in YOLOv3).
3. Dividing the output feature map into an $S \times S$ **grid** (e.g., $7 \times 7$ in v1, $13 \times 13$ in v3).
4. Each grid cell predicts:
   - $B$ bounding boxes, each with $(x, y, w, h)$ and a **confidence score**.
   - $C$ **conditional class probabilities** (one per class).
5. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** removes duplicate detections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why YOLO is faster:** The entire detection is a single regression problem — only **one forward pass** per image. Faster R-CNN requires ~2000 proposals per image, each processed by the second-stage head.

---

## 2.2 The Loss Function (Multi-Task Loss)

Both YOLO and Faster R-CNN train using a **multi-task loss** combining classification, localization, and confidence.

### YOLOv1 Loss (Foundational Formula)

The total loss is the sum of **localization loss** + **confidence loss** + **classification loss**:

$$
L = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} \left[ (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 + (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + (\sqrt{w_i} - \sqrt{\hat{w}_i})^2 + (\sqrt{h_i} - \sqrt{\hat{h}_i})^2 \right]
$$

$$
+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2
$$

$$
+ \lambda_{\text{noobj}} \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \sum_{j=0}^{B} \mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{noobj}} (C_i - \hat{C}_i)^2
$$

$$
+ \sum_{i=0}^{S^2} \mathbb{1}_{i}^{\text{obj}} \sum_{c \in \text{classes}} (p_i(c) - \hat{p}_i(c))^2
$$

Where:
- $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}}$ = 1 if the $j$-th box of cell $i$ is responsible for predicting an object, else 0.
- $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ and $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ (standard weighting).
- $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ is used so that small-box errors are penalized more heavily.

### Faster R-CNN Multi-Task Loss

$$
L(\{p_i\}, \{t_i\}) = \frac{1}{N_{\text{cls}}} \sum_i L_{\text{cls}}(p_i, p_i^*) + \lambda \frac{1}{N_{\text{reg}}} \sum_i p_i^* L_{\text{reg}}(t_i, t_i^*)
$$

Where:
- $p_i$ = predicted probability of objectness for anchor $i$.
- $p_i^*$ = 1 if anchor is positive (IoU $\geq$ 0.7 with GT), 0 if negative (IoU $\leq$ 0.3).
- $t_i = (t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ = predicted box regression deltas.
- $L_{\text{cls}}$ = **log loss** (binary cross-entropy) for object vs. background.
- $L_{\text{reg}}$ = **Smooth L1 loss** (robust to outliers).
- $\lambda$ = balancing weight (typically 10).

---

## 2.3 The Metric Engine — IoU and mAP

### Intersection over Union (IoU)

Given a **predicted box** $B_p$ and a **ground-truth box** $B_{gt}$:

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_{gt})}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_{gt})}
$$

A detection is considered a **True Positive (TP)** if $\text{IoU} \geq \tau$ (threshold), where $\tau$ is **0.5** for PASCAL VOC and averaged over $\{0.5, 0.55, \dots, 0.95\}$ for COCO.

### Precision and Recall

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

### Average Precision (AP) and mAP

For each class, sort detections by descending confidence, compute the **Precision-Recall (PR) curve**, and:

$$
AP = \int_0^1 p(r) \, dr
$$

In practice, this is computed as the **area under the PR curve** using the **11-point interpolation** (VOC2007) or **all-point interpolation** (VOC2010+).

The final metric is:

$$
mAP = \frac{1}{N_{\text{classes}}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{classes}}} AP_i
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are examinable in both Part A and Part B of KTU ESE questions.**

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **IoU** | $\dfrac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_{gt})}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_{gt})}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq \text{IoU} \leq 1$ |
| **Precision** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Dimensionless, $0$ to $1$ |
| **Recall** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless, $0$ to $1$ |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Dimensionless, $0$ to $1$ |
| **AP** | $\int_0^1 p(r) \, dr$ (area under PR curve) | Dimensionless, $0$ to $1$ |
| **mAP** | $\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} AP_i$ | Dimensionless, $0$ to $1$ |
| **mAP@0.5** | mAP at single IoU threshold $\tau = 0.5$ | PASCAL VOC standard |
| **mAP@0.5:0.95** | Mean of mAP over $\tau \in \{0.5, 0.55, \dots, 0.95\}$ | **COCO** primary metric |
| **Anchor Scales** | Typical values $\in \{128^2, 256^2, 512^2\}$ | Pixels$^2$ |
| **Anchor Ratios** | Typical values $\in \{1:1, 1:2, 2:1\}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Smooth L1** | $0.5 x^2$ if $\vert x \vert < 1$, else $\vert x \vert - 0.5$ | Loss value |
| **NMS Threshold** | Typically **0.5** IoU to suppress duplicates | Dimensionless |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles** (Tesla, Waymo) use YOLO variants for real-time pedestrian and obstacle detection.
- **Medical Imaging**: Faster R-CNN detects tumors in MRI/CT scans (sacrifices speed for accuracy).
- **Industrial Defect Detection** on assembly lines (e.g., PCB fault detection).
- **Surveillance & Crowd Analytics** in airports and stadiums.
- **Agricultural Robotics**: fruit/vegetable detection and yield estimation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Selection Rule of Thumb:**
> - Need **real-time** ($\geq 30$ FPS)? → Choose **YOLO** (or SSD, EfficientDet).
> - Need **highest accuracy** (e.g., medical imaging) and can tolerate latency? → Choose **Faster R-CNN** (or Cascade R-CNN).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: IoU Calculation in Coordinates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU students frequently lose marks by failing to derive the IoU formula from raw bounding-box coordinates. This is a high-weight derivation.**

**Given:** Two axis-aligned bounding boxes.

- Predicted box: $B_p = (x_{p1}, y_{p1}, x_{p2}, y_{p2})$ where $(x_{p1}, y_{p1})$ is the top-left corner and $(x_{p2}, y_{p2})$ is the bottom-right corner.
- Ground-truth box: $B_{gt} = (x_{g1}, y_{g1}, x_{g2}, y_{g2})$ in the same convention.

### Step 1 — Compute the Coordinates of the Intersection Rectangle

The top-left corner of the intersection is the **maximum** of the two top-left corners:

$$
x_{\text{inter}_1} = \max(x_{p1}, x_{g1}), \qquad y_{\text{inter}_1} = \max(y_{p1}, y_{g1})
$$

The bottom-right corner is the **minimum** of the two bottom-right corners:

$$
x_{\text{inter}_2} = \min(x_{p2}, x_{g2}), \qquad y_{\text{inter}_2} = \min(y_{p2}, y_{g2})
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Intersection Area

The intersection area is non-zero **only if** $x_{\text{inter}_2} > x_{\text{inter}_1}$ **and** $y_{\text{inter}_2} > y_{\text{inter}_1}$. The width and height of the intersection are:

$$
w_{\text{inter}} = \max(0, x_{\text{inter}_2} - x_{\text{inter}_1})
$$

$$
h_{\text{inter}} = \max(0, y_{\text{inter}_2} - y_{\text{inter}_1})
$$

Therefore:

$$
A_{\text{inter}} = w_{\text{inter}} \cdot h_{\text{inter}}
$$

The $\max(0, \cdot)$ term ensures that if the boxes do not overlap, the intersection area is correctly **clamped to 0**.

### Step 3 — Compute Each Box's Individual Area

$$
A_p = (x_{p2} - x_{p1}) \cdot (y_{p2} - y_{p1})
$$

$$
A_{gt} = (x_{g2} - x_{g1}) \cdot (y_{g2} - y_{g1})
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Union Area (Inclusion–Exclusion)

$$
A_{\text{union}} = A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}
$$

This is the standard set-theoretic identity: $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$.

### Step 5 — Final IoU

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}} = \frac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Predicted box** $B_p = (10, 10, 50, 50)$.
**Ground-truth box** $B_{gt} = (30, 30, 70, 70)$.

**Intersection coordinates:**

$$
x_{\text{inter}_1} = \max(10, 30) = 30, \quad y_{\text{inter}_1} = \max(10, 30) = 30
$$

$$
x_{\text{inter}_2} = \min(50, 70) = 50, \quad y_{\text{inter}_2} = \min(50, 70) = 50
$$

**Intersection area:**

$$
w_{\text{inter}} = 50 - 30 = 20, \quad h_{\text{inter}} = 50 - 30 = 20
$$

$$
A_{\text{inter}} = 20 \times 20 = 400
$$

**Individual areas:**

$$
A_p = (50-10)(50-10) = 40 \times 40 = 1600
$$

$$
A_{gt} = (70-30)(70-30) = 40 \times 40 = 1600
$$

**Union area:**

$$
A_{\text{union}} = 1600 + 1600 - 400 = 2800
$$

**Final IoU:**

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{400}{2800} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.143
$$

This IoU is **less than 0.5**, so at the standard PASCAL VOC threshold, this detection would be classified as a **False Positive**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Precision-Recall Curve and AP

### Step 1 — Rank Detections by Confidence

Each predicted box from the model is assigned a **confidence score** (e.g., the product of objectness probability and class probability in YOLO). Sort all detections for a given class in **descending order of confidence**.

### Step 2 — Walk Down the Ranked List

Iterate through detections one by one. For each detection $d$ at rank $k$:

- If $d$ matches a **ground-truth object** (IoU $\geq \tau$) not yet matched → it is a **TP**, mark that GT as used.
- Else → it is a **FP**.

### Step 3 — Maintain Running Precision and Recall

At each step $k$, with $TP(k)$ true positives and $FP(k)$ false positives so far:

$$
P(k) = \frac{TP(k)}{TP(k) + FP(k)}
$$

$$
R(k) = \frac{TP(k)}{\text{Total GT objects of this class}}
$$

### Step 4 — All-Point AP Interpolation (VOC 2010+)

For every unique recall value $r$, set $P(r)$ to the **maximum precision** at any recall $\geq r$:

$$
P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{r' \,:\, r' \geq r} P(r')
$$

### Step 5 — Area Under the Interpolated Curve

$$
AP = \sum_{r \in \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_N\}} (r_{i+1} - r_i) \cdot P_{\text{interp}}(r_{i+1})
$$

Where $\{r_i\}$ are the sorted unique recall values encountered along the PR curve.

### Step 6 — mAP Across Classes

$$
mAP = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} AP_c
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (Single Class)

Suppose a class has **5 ground-truth objects**. The model produces the following ranked detections:

| Rank | Confidence | IoU with GT | Result | Running TP | Running FP | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.95 | 0.82 | TP | 1 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.20 |
| 2 | 0.90 | 0.61 | TP | 2 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.40 |
| 3 | 0.85 | 0.34 | FP | 2 | 1 | 0.667 | 0.40 |
| 4 | 0.80 | 0.74 | TP | 3 | 1 | 0.750 | 0.60 |
| 5 | 0.70 | 0.20 | FP | 3 | 2 | 0.600 | 0.60 |
| 6 | 0.65 | 0.55 | TP | 4 | 2 | 0.667 | 0.80 |
| 7 | 0.60 | 0.15 | FP | 4 | 3 | 0.571 | 0.80 |

**Computing AP (all-point interpolation):**

Unique recall values encountered: $\{0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8\}$.

Precision at each (and max-from-that-point-forward):

- $r = 0.2$: $P_{\text{interp}} = \max\{1.000, 1.000, 0.667, 0.750, 0.600, 0.667, 0.571\} = 1.000$
- $r = 0.4$: $P_{\text{interp}} = \max\{1.000, 0.667, 0.750, 0.600, 0.667, 0.571\} = 1.000$
- $r = 0.6$: $P_{\text{interp}} = \max\{0.750, 0.600, 0.667, 0.571\} = 0.750$
- $r = 0.8$: $P_{\text{interp}} = \max\{0.667, 0.571\} = 0.667$

Summing the rectangles:

$$
AP = (0.4 - 0.2)(1.000) + (0.6 - 0.4)(1.000) + (0.8 - 0.6)(0.750) + (1.0 - 0.8)(0.667)
$$

$$
AP = (0.2)(1.000) + (0.2)(1.000) + (0.2)(0.750) + (0.2)(0.667)
$$

$$
AP = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.150 + 0.133 = 0.683
$$

If this were the only class, then **mAP = 0.683** at IoU threshold $\tau = 0.5$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Loss Function Comparison

| Aspect | YOLOv1 Loss | Faster R-CNN Loss |
|---|---|---|
| **Components** | Coord + Confidence (obj) + Confidence (no-obj) + Class | Classification (RPN + Detection) + Regression (RPN + Detection) |
| **Box Encoding** | Direct $(x, y, \sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h})$ regression | Anchor-relative $(t_x, t_y, t_w, t_h)$ deltas |
| **Box Penalization** | Sum-of-squares (MSE) | Smooth L1 (Huber-like) |
| **Class Loss** | Sum-of-squares | Log loss / cross-entropy |
| **Weighting** | $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$, $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$ | $\lambda = 10$ (typical) |
| **Normalization** | None (sum over grid cells) | $1/N_{\text{cls}}$ and $1/N_{\text{reg}}$ |
| **Handling Small Boxes** | $\sqrt{w}, \sqrt{h}$ partial fix | Anchor-relative offsets fix |
| **Differentiable?** | Yes | Yes (Smooth L1) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture — Faster R-CNN

The following Mermaid block diagrams the two-stage Faster R-CNN pipeline, from raw image input to final detection output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Image] --> B[CNN Backbone e.g. ResNet-101]
    B --> C[Convolutional Feature Map]
    C --> D[Region Proposal Network RPN]
    D --> E{Anchor Box at each location k=9}
    E --> F[Objectness Score 2k outputs]
    E --> G[Bounding Box Deltas 4k outputs]
    F --> H[Apply NMS and Top-K filtering]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Region Proposals ~2000 boxes]
    I --> J[RoI Pooling Fixed 7x7]
    J --> K[Fully Connected Layers]
    K --> L[Softmax Classifier C+1 classes]
    K --> M[Bounding Box Regressor 4C offsets]
    L --> N[Final Detections Class + Box + Score]
    M --> N
```

**Explanation of the block flow:**
- **CNN Backbone** extracts deep features while preserving spatial information.
- **RPN** uses a $3 \times 3$ sliding window and $1 \times 1$ convolutions to predict objectness and box deltas for $k = 9$ anchors per location.
- **RoI Pooling** warps variable-size proposal features into a uniform $7 \times 7$ grid for the FC head.
- The FC head produces final **per-class** classification scores and bounding-box refinements.

---

## 4.2 High-Level Architecture — YOLO (Unified Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Image 448x448] --> B[Single CNN Backbone Darknet-53]
    B --> C[Feature Map 13x13 grid for YOLOv3]
    C --> D[Per Grid Cell Prediction]
    D --> E[B bounding boxes with x, y, w, h, confidence]
    D --> F[C conditional class probabilities]
    E --> G[Reshape Tensor S x S x Bx5 + C]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Confidence Thresholding e.g. 0.5]
    H --> I[Non-Maximum Suppression IoU threshold 0.5]
    I --> J[Final Detections]
```

**Explanation of the block flow:**
- The **entire detection is one forward pass**, hence the name "You Only Look Once".
- Each cell of the $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $13 \times 13$) predicts $B$ boxes (typically 3 in YOLOv3, one per anchor scale).
- The output tensor has shape $S \times S \times (B \times 5 + C)$ where 5 = $(x, y, w, h, \text{confidence})$ and $C$ = number of classes.
- **NMS** removes redundant boxes detecting the same object.

---

## 4.3 Training Pipeline — Custom Dataset Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Collect Raw Images] --> B[Annotate Boxes and Classes]
    B --> C{Annotation Format}
    C --> D[YOLO txt per image]
    C --> E[VOC XML per image]
    C --> F[COCO JSON dataset]
    D --> G[Convert to Model Format]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Split Train 80 percent Val 10 percent Test 10 percent]
    H --> I[Data Augmentation Flip Mosaic MixUp]
    I --> J[Initialize Backbone with Pre-trained Weights]
    J --> K[Train End-to-End Multi-Task Loss]
    K --> L{Validation mAP plateaus}
    L -->|No| K
    L -->|Yes| M[Save Best Checkpoint]
    M --> N[Evaluate on Test Set]
    N --> O[Compute mAP at 0.5 and 0.5 to 0.95]
    O --> P[Report FPS Latency and Model Size]
```

---

## 4.4 Evaluation Pipeline — Standard Metrics Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Test Set Images] --> B[Run Inference]
    B --> C[Raw Predictions Boxes Scores Classes]
    C --> D[Load Ground Truth Annotations]
    D --> E[Match Predictions to GT via IoU]
    E --> F{IoU greater than or equal to threshold tau}
    F -->|Yes| G[True Positive TP]
    F -->|No| H[False Positive FP]
    C --> I[Unmatched GT objects]
    I --> J[False Negative FN]
    G --> K[Compute Precision and Recall per class]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Plot PR Curve]
    L --> M[Compute AP per class Area under curve]
    M --> N[Average AP across classes to get mAP]
    N --> O[Repeat for IoU thresholds 0.5 to 0.95]
    O --> P[Final Report mAP at 0.5, mAP at 0.5 to 0.95, FPS]
```

---

## 4.5 Comparison Block — One-Stage vs. Two-Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[CNN-based Object Detection] --> B[Two-Stage e.g. Faster R-CNN]
    A --> C[One-Stage e.g. YOLO SSD]
    B --> D[Region Proposal First]
    B --> E[Classification Second]
    C --> F[Direct Regression in One Pass]
    D --> G[Higher Accuracy slower ~7 FPS]
    F --> H[Faster speed ~45 to 155 FPS]
    G --> I[Use Case Medical Imaging Surveillance]
    H --> J[Use Case Autonomous Driving Robotics]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between object classification, object localization, and object detection. State one real-world application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Object Classification** assigns a single class label to an entire image (e.g., "this image contains a cat"). It answers "**what** is in the image?" but does not specify location. *Application:* Image tagging on social media platforms.
- **Object Localization** identifies the position of **one** dominant object by drawing a single bounding box, but does not provide a class label. It answers "**where** is the object?" *Application:* Centering the camera on a detected face in a smartphone.
- **Object Detection** performs both classification **and** localization for **multiple** objects in a single image. It answers "**what** is in the image and **where**?" *Application:* Real-time pedestrian detection in autonomous vehicles.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric. With a neat diagram, explain the case when IoU = 0 and IoU = 1.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** IoU is the ratio of the area of overlap between a predicted bounding box $B_p$ and a ground-truth box $B_{gt}$ to the area of their union.

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{Area}(B_p \cap B_{gt})}{\text{Area}(B_p \cup B_{gt})}, \quad 0 \leq \text{IoU} \leq 1
$$

**Case IoU = 0:** The two bounding boxes do **not overlap at all** (disjoint). The intersection area is 0, so $\text{IoU} = 0 / (\text{union area}) = 0$.

**Case IoU = 1:** The two bounding boxes are **perfectly aligned** (identical). The intersection area equals the union area, so $\text{IoU} = A / A = 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Faster R-CNN** architecture. Discuss the role of the **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** and **anchor boxes** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A custom object detection dataset has 3 classes: *cat, dog, car*. The model produces the following ranked detections for the *dog* class (8 ground-truth dogs in the test image):

| Rank | Confidence | IoU | Match |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.98 | 0.85 | TP |
| 2 | 0.95 | 0.72 | TP |
| 3 | 0.90 | 0.42 | FP |
| 4 | 0.88 | 0.78 | TP |
| 5 | 0.82 | 0.65 | TP |
| 6 | 0.75 | 0.30 | FP |
| 7 | 0.70 | 0.80 | TP |
| 8 | 0.65 | 0.25 | FP |

Compute the **mAP@0.5** for the dog class using the all-point interpolation method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Faster R-CNN Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **Input Image → CNN Backbone (1 mark):** The image is fed to a pre-trained CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet-101). The backbone produces a **convolutional feature map** that encodes both semantic and spatial information.

2. **Region Proposal Network (RPN) (3 marks):** The RPN is a small fully-convolutional network that slides a $3 \times 3$ window over the feature map. At each spatial location, it evaluates **$k = 9$ anchor boxes** (3 scales $\times$ 3 aspect ratios: $\{1:1, 1:2, 2:1\}$). The RPN has two sibling $1 \times 1$ convolutional outputs:
   - **Classification layer:** $2k = 18$ scores per location (object / not-object, softmax).
   - **Regression layer:** $4k = 36$ values per location (box-center and box-size deltas relative to each anchor).

   Non-Maximum Suppression is applied to the proposals, and the top ~2000 are passed forward.

3. **RoI Pooling and Detection Head (2 marks):** Each variable-size proposal is converted into a **fixed $7 \times 7 \times 512$ feature map** via RoI Pooling. These are flattened, passed through fully-connected layers, and produce:
   - **Softmax classifier** over $C + 1$ classes (including background).
   - **Bounding-box regressor** with $4C$ outputs (one box per class).

4. **Multi-task Loss:** Training minimizes the combined classification + regression loss for **both** the RPN and the detection head, with anchor-to-GT assignment based on IoU ($\geq 0.7$ = positive, $\leq 0.3$ = negative).

**Part (b) — Computing mAP@0.5 (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Build the ranked list (1 mark):**

Total GT objects for the *dog* class: **8**.

| Rank | Confidence | Result | Running TP | Running FP | Precision $P$ | Recall $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.98 | TP | 1 | 0 | 1/1 = 1.000 | 1/8 = 0.125 |
| 2 | 0.95 | TP | 2 | 0 | 2/2 = 1.000 | 2/8 = 0.250 |
| 3 | 0.90 | FP | 2 | 1 | 2/3 = 0.667 | 2/8 = 0.250 |
| 4 | 0.88 | TP | 3 | 1 | 3/4 = 0.750 | 3/8 = 0.375 |
| 5 | 0.82 | TP | 4 | 1 | 4/5 = 0.800 | 4/8 = 0.500 |
| 6 | 0.75 | FP | 4 | 2 | 4/6 = 0.667 | 4/8 = 0.500 |
| 7 | 0.70 | TP | 5 | 2 | 5/7 = 0.714 | 5/8 = 0.625 |
| 8 | 0.65 | FP | 5 | 3 | 5/8 = 0.625 | 5/8 = 0.625 |

**Step 2 — Identify unique recall values (1 mark):**

Unique recall values encountered: $\{0.125, 0.250, 0.375, 0.500, 0.625\}$.

**Step 3 — All-point interpolation (3 marks):**

For each recall $r$, $P_{\text{interp}}(r) = \max_{r' \geq r} P(r')$:

- $r = 0.125$: max of all precisions $\geq 0.125$ recall $\to \max\{1.000, 1.000, 0.667, 0.750, 0.800, 0.667, 0.714, 0.625\} = 1.000$
- $r = 0.250$: $\max\{1.000, 0.667, 0.750, 0.800, 0.667, 0.714, 0.625\} = 1.000$
- $r = 0.375$: $\max\{0.750, 0.800, 0.667, 0.714, 0.625\} = 0.800$
- $r = 0.500$: $\max\{0.800, 0.667, 0.714, 0.625\} = 0.800$
- $r = 0.625$: $\max\{0.714, 0.625\} = 0.714$

**Step 4 — Compute AP as area under interpolated curve (2 marks):**

$$
AP = (0.250 - 0.125)(1.000) + (0.375 - 0.250)(1.000) + (0.500 - 0.375)(0.800) + (0.625 - 0.500)(0.800)
$$

$$
AP = (0.125)(1.000) + (0.125)(1.000) + (0.125)(0.800) + (0.125)(0.800)
$$

$$
AP = 0.125 + 0.125 + 0.100 + 0.100 = 0.450
$$

**Step 5 — Final mAP@0.5 (since only 1 class shown):**

$$
mAP@0.5 = 0.450
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **YOLO** object detection algorithm. Compare its architecture and performance trade-offs with Faster R-CNN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During training of a YOLO-style detector, the model produces the following parameters for a single grid cell: predicted box $(x, y, w, h) = (0.4, 0.6, 0.5, 0.3)$, ground truth $(x_{gt}, y_{gt}, w_{gt}, h_{gt}) = (0.45, 0.55, 0.4, 0.4)$, predicted confidence $\hat{C} = 0.8$, ground-truth confidence $C = 1$, predicted class probability $\hat{p}(\text{dog}) = 0.7$, ground-truth $p(\text{dog}) = 1$. Use $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ and $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$. Compute the **total loss** for this cell. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — YOLO vs. Faster R-CNN (7 marks):**

1. **YOLO Concept (2 marks):** YOLO (You Only Look Once) reformulates object detection as a **single regression problem**. The input image (resized to $448 \times 448$) is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $7 \times 7$ in v1). Each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes (each with $x, y, w, h$ and a confidence score) and $C$ conditional class probabilities — all in **one CNN forward pass**.

2. **Architecture Pipeline (2 marks):** Image → Single CNN (Darknet-19/53) → Feature map → $1 \times 1$ conv head producing an output tensor of shape $S \times S \times (B \times 5 + C)$ → NMS → Final detections.

3. **Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | YOLO | Faster R-CNN |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | One-stage | Two-stage |
| **Speed (FPS)** | 45–155 (real-time) | ~7 (slow) |
| **mAP (VOC 2007)** | ~78% (v3) | ~78% (similar) |
| **Region Proposals** | None (grid-based) | ~2000 per image (RPN) |
| **Best For** | Real-time, video | High accuracy, small objects |
| **Localization** | Less precise (coarse grid) | More precise (refinement stage) |
| **Small Object Detection** | Weaker | Stronger |
| **Training Stability** | Sensitive to anchor choices | More stable |

**Part (b) — YOLO Loss Calculation (7 marks):**

The total loss has four terms. We assume the box is "responsible" (i.e., $\mathbb{1}_{ij}^{\text{obj}} = 1$).

**Step 1 — Localization loss (2 marks):**

$$
L_{\text{coord}} = (x - x_{gt})^2 + (y - y_{gt})^2 + (\sqrt{w} - \sqrt{w_{gt}})^2 + (\sqrt{h} - \sqrt{h_{gt}})^2
$$

$$
= (0.4 - 0.45)^2 + (0.6 - 0.55)^2 + (\sqrt{0.5} - \sqrt{0.4})^2 + (\sqrt{0.3} - \sqrt{0.4})^2
$$

$$
= (-0.05)^2 + (0.05)^2 + (0.7071 - 0.6325)^2 + (0.5477 - 0.6325)^2
$$

$$
= 0.0025 + 0.0025 + (0.0746)^2 + (-0.0848)^2
$$

$$
= 0.0025 + 0.0025 + 0.00557 + 0.00719
$$

$$
L_{\text{coord}} = 0.01776
$$

**Step 2 — Weighted localization contribution (1 mark):**

$$
L_{\text{coord, weighted}} = \lambda_{\text{coord}} \cdot L_{\text{coord}} = 5 \times 0.01776 = 0.0888
$$

**Step 3 — Object confidence loss (1 mark):**

$$
L_{\text{obj}} = (C - \hat{C})^2 = (1 - 0.8)^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04
$$

**Step 4 — Classification loss (1 mark):**

$$
L_{\text{cls}} = (p(\text{dog}) - \hat{p}(\text{dog}))^2 = (1 - 0.7)^2 = 0.09
$$

**Step 5 — No-object and final total (2 marks):**

Since we are told to compute the loss for a single cell that contains an object, the no-object term does not apply (or applies with $\mathbb{1}^{\text{noobj}} = 0$).

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{coord, weighted}} + L_{\text{obj}} + L_{\text{cls}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 0.0888 + 0.04 + 0.09
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 0.2188
$$

**Result:** The total YOLO loss for this cell is **0.2188** (dimensionless).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE MARKS in object detection questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AP with mAP** (3-mark questions): AP is for a **single class**; mAP is the **mean** over all classes. Forgetting the mean averaging step costs 1 mark.
> 2. **IoU formula mistakes**: Forgetting to subtract the intersection from the union. The correct formula is $A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}$, **not** $A_p + A_{gt}$.
> 3. **YOLO loss weighting**: Students often forget the $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$ multiplier on the localization term. Without it, the loss is dominated by classification and the model fails to learn localization.
> 4. **NMS step omission**: In design questions, students describe YOLO/RCNN but forget to mention **Non-Maximum Suppression** as the post-processing step that removes duplicate boxes.
> 5. **Square-root trick in YOLO**: Examiners specifically test $(\sqrt{w} - \sqrt{w_{gt}})^2$. Writing $(w - w_{gt})^2$ directly costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Anchor-box to GT matching**: Students should explicitly state the IoU thresholds (0.7 positive, 0.3 negative) used in Faster R-CNN's anchor labelling.
> 7. **COCO mAP range confusion**: PASCAL VOC uses a single threshold (0.5); COCO's primary metric **mAP@[0.5:0.95]** averages over 10 thresholds — students often report only one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for the KTU lab viva and ESE.**

- **Object Detection = Classification + Localization**, both performed simultaneously.
- **Faster R-CNN** is a **two-stage** detector: (1) RPN proposes regions, (2) Detection head classifies + refines.
- **YOLO** is a **one-stage** detector: a single CNN directly regresses boxes and classes.
- **Backbone networks** (VGG, ResNet, Darknet) provide pre-trained feature extraction; transfer learning is standard.
- **Anchor boxes** are pre-defined reference rectangles (typically $k = 9$ in Faster R-CNN) used to parameterize predicted boxes.
- **RPN** is a fully-convolutional network with $1 \times 1$ conv heads producing $2k$ objectness scores and $4k$ box deltas.
- **RoI Pooling** converts variable-size proposal features to a fixed $7 \times 7$ grid for the FC classifier.
- **IoU formula** (always memorize): $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}}$.
- **A detection is TP if IoU $\geq$ 0.5** (VOC) or over averaged thresholds (COCO).
- **Precision-Recall Curve** is built by sorting detections by confidence and computing running $P$ and $R$.
- **AP = area under the interpolated PR curve** (all-point or 11-point).
- **mAP = mean of AP across all classes**, the universal detection benchmark.
- **YOLO loss =** $\lambda_{\text{coord}} L_{\text{coord}} + L_{\text{obj}} + \lambda_{\text{noobj}} L_{\text{noobj}} + L_{\text{cls}}$, with $\lambda_{\text{coord}} = 5$, $\lambda_{\text{noobj}} = 0.5$.
- **Smooth L1 loss** is used in Faster R-CNN for robust bounding-box regression.
- **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** is the standard post-processing to remove duplicate detections.
- **Data augmentation** (flip, mosaic, mixup, color jitter) is critical for training on small custom datasets.
- **Pre-trained weights on COCO** are the de-facto initialization for transfer learning to custom datasets.
- **Evaluation metrics to report**: **mAP@0.5**, **mAP@[0.5:0.95]**, **FPS (speed)**, **model size (MB)**, and **per-class AP**.
- **Speed vs Accuracy trade-off**: YOLO for real-time, Faster R-CNN for high accuracy.
- **Standard lab workflow**: Annotate $\to$ Convert format $\to$ Augment $\to$ Train $\to$ Validate $\to$ Test $\to$ Compute mAP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the lab record, always include (1) a brief architecture diagram, (2) the loss curve plot (training vs. validation), (3) a sample prediction visualization with bounding boxes, and (4) a table of mAP scores per class. Examiners explicitly award marks for these four components.

topics.title: Image Segmentation: Implement a CNN-based image segmentation model (e.g., U-Net/DeepLabv3) for semantic or instance segmentation. Evaluate performance using standard metrics.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL508 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Image Segmentation: Implement a CNN-based image segmentation model (e.g., U-Net/DeepLabv3) for semantic or instance segmentation. Evaluate performance using standard metrics.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image Segmentation** is a computer vision task that partitions a digital image into multiple meaningful segments (sets of pixels, also called *image objects*). The goal of pixel-level classification is to assign a class label to **every pixel** in the image, producing a segmentation mask of the same spatial resolution as the input.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRAL508 — Advanced AI & ML Lab), the experiment focuses on two principal paradigms:

| Paradigm | Granularity | Output | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Segmentation** | Class-level | One mask channel per class | Road, sky, building detection |
| **Instance Segmentation** | Object-level | Mask + per-instance ID | Counting cells, pedestrian ID |
| **Panoptic Segmentation** | Hybrid (semantic + instance) | Both combined | Autonomous driving scenes |

The canonical architectures mandated by the syllabus are:

- **U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015)** — an *encoder–decoder* symmetric network with *skip connections*, originally designed for biomedical image segmentation.
- **DeepLabv3 (Chen et al., 2017)** — a *dilated (atrous) convolution*-based network that uses **Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP)** to capture multi-scale context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction for exam:** *Classification* outputs one label per image. *Segmentation* outputs one label per pixel. The model must therefore preserve spatial dimensions end-to-end.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **child's coloring book illustrator**:
- A *classification CNN* (e.g., ResNet) tells you "this whole picture is a dog." 🐶
- A *semantic segmentation* model hands you a **pre-drawn coloring template** where every pixel is pre-labeled "dog," "grass," or "sky," and you only need to fill in the right color in each pre-defined region.
- An *instance segmentation* model goes further: if there are **two dogs** in the picture, it differentiates *Dog-1* (orange) and *Dog-2* (blue), even though they belong to the same class.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Analogy — Autonomous Driving:** A self-driving car uses semantic segmentation to label every pixel as *drivable-road, lane-marking, pedestrian, vehicle, sidewalk*. This pixel-precise understanding is critical for collision avoidance — a bounding box is too coarse when a pedestrian is 2 meters away.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold-highlighted for emphasis)

- **IoU (Intersection over Union / Jaccard Index)** — the **gold standard** segmentation metric.
- **Dice Coefficient (F1-score for sets)** — closely related to IoU.
- **Pixel Accuracy** — simplest but *misleading* on imbalanced classes.
- **mAP (mean Average Precision)** — used in instance segmentation (Mask R-CNN).
- **Boundary F1 (BF1) / Hausdorff Distance** — boundary-aware metrics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 U-Net Architecture — Operational Walkthrough

U-Net has a symmetric "U"-shaped topology. It has **two paths**:

### Encoder (Contracting Path)
- Repeated application of two **3×3 convolutions**, each followed by **ReLU** and **Batch Normalization**.
- A **2×2 max-pooling** operation with stride 2 for downsampling.
- At each downsampling step, the number of feature channels is **doubled** (64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 1024).
- Purpose: capture *context* (what is in the image).

### Bottleneck
- The deepest layer (1024 channels) where global context is encoded.

### Decoder (Expanding Path)
- **2×2 transposed convolution (up-conv)** for upsampling, halving the number of channels.
- **Skip connections**: feature maps from the encoder at the *same resolution* are cropped and concatenated with the decoder feature maps. This recovers *spatial precision* lost during downsampling.
- Two 3×3 convolutions + ReLU after each concatenation.
- Purpose: enable *precise localization* (where the object is).

### Final Layer
- A **1×1 convolution** maps the 64-channel feature map to the desired number of classes $C$.
- For binary segmentation: $C = 1$ with **sigmoid** activation.
- For multi-class: $C = N_{\text{classes}}$ with **softmax** activation.

## 2.2 DeepLabv3 — Operational Walkthrough

DeepLabv3 is fundamentally different from U-Net — it does **not** use an encoder–decoder topology. Instead, it uses:

- **Atrous (Dilated) Convolution**: increases receptive field *without* reducing spatial resolution.
- **ASPP (Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling)**: applies **atrous convolutions at multiple dilation rates** (e.g., 6, 12, 18) **in parallel**, then concatenates the outputs. This captures objects at multiple scales.
- **Image-level pooling (GAP)** branch added in DeepLabv3 for global context.
- Backbone: typically **ResNet-101** (or Xception, MobileNet) with atrous convolutions replacing the last few pooling layers (output stride = 8 or 16 instead of 32).

## 2.3 Loss Functions

| Loss | Formula (per pixel) | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** | $-\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]$ | Balanced binary masks |
| **Categorical Cross-Entropy** | $-\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Multi-class segmentation |
| **Dice Loss** | $1 - \dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert + \epsilon}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert + \epsilon}$ | Imbalanced masks (medical) |
| **Focal Loss** | $-\alpha (1-\hat{y})^\gamma \log(\hat{y})$ | Severe class imbalance |
| **Combo (BCE + Dice)** | $L_{BCE} + L_{Dice}$ | Robust default in practice |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked in KTU ESE viva and written exams** for PCRAL508.

| # | Metric / Equation | Formula | Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pixel Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| 2 | **Mean Pixel Accuracy (MPA)** | $\dfrac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} \dfrac{TP_c}{TP_c + FN_c}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Per-class averaged |
| 3 | **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard)** | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **Primary KTU metric** |
| 4 | **Mean IoU (mIoU)** | $\dfrac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} IoU_c$ | $[0, 1]$ | PASCAL VOC standard |
| 5 | **Dice Coefficient (F1)** | $Dice = \dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert} = \dfrac{2 \, TP}{2 \, TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Medical imaging standard |
| 6 | **Dice ↔ IoU Relation** | $Dice = \dfrac{2 \, IoU}{1 + IoU}$ | — | Algebraic equivalence |
| 7 | **Boundary F1 (BF1)** | $F1$ of boundary pixels within tolerance $\tau$ | $[0, 1]$ | Cityscapes metric |
| 8 | **Hausdorff Distance** | $\max\!\left(\max_{p \in P}\min_{g \in G} d(p,g),\ \max_{g \in G}\min_{p \in P} d(g,p)\right)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Worst-case boundary error |
| 9 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $-\sum_c y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Penalizes confident errors |
| 10 | **Dice Loss** | $1 - Dice$ | $[0, 1]$ | Gradient-stable form (see §3) |
| 11 | **Atrous Conv RF growth** | $RF_{eff} = k + (k-1)(d-1)$ | — | For kernel $k$, dilation $d$ |
| 12 | **Output Stride** | $OS = \dfrac{\text{Input size}}{\text{Feature map size}}$ | — | OS=8 for DeepLabv3+ fine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for exam:** *"TP is in the numerator twice for Dice, once for IoU."* The extra $TP$ in Dice rewards overlap more leniently — this is why Dice is preferred for small structures (e.g., tumors).

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT)**: U-Net is the de-facto standard for tumor, organ, and lesion segmentation.
- **Autonomous Driving**: DeepLabv3 / DeepLabv3+ powers semantic scene understanding in datasets like Cityscapes and KITTI.
- **Agriculture**: Drone-based crop/weed segmentation for precision spraying.
- **Satellite Imagery**: Building footprint extraction, flood mapping.
- **Industrial Defect Detection**: Pixel-level defect localization in manufacturing.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dice Coefficient from Set Theory

The Dice coefficient measures the overlap between two sets: the **predicted** set $P$ and the **ground truth** set $G$.

**Step 1 — Define overlap cardinalities.**
Let $TP = \vert P \cap G \vert$ (true positives: predicted and actually positive).
Let $FP = \vert P \setminus G \vert$ (predicted positive, actually negative).
Let $FN = \vert G \setminus P \vert$ (predicted negative, actually positive).

**Step 2 — Express set cardinalities.**

$$
\vert P \vert = TP + FP
$$

$$
\vert G \vert = TP + FN
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Dice definition.**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}
$$

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \, TP}{(TP + FP) + (TP + FN)}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad Dice = \frac{2 \, TP}{2 \, TP + FP + FN}
$$

This is the **set-based form** of the F1-score applied to masks.

**Step 4 — Convert to a soft (differentiable) form for backpropagation.**
Since masks are real-valued probability maps $\hat{Y}$ and ground truth $Y$, we replace set operations with element-wise sums over $N$ pixels:

$$
Dice_{soft} = \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{y}_i + \epsilon}
$$

The smoothing constant $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-6}$) prevents division by zero when both numerator and denominator are zero (empty mask).

**Step 5 — Dice Loss.**

$$
L_{Dice} = 1 - Dice_{soft}
$$

This is minimized when $Dice_{soft} \to 1$ (perfect overlap).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: IoU ↔ Dice Algebraic Equivalence

Given the set form of IoU:

$$
IoU = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}
$$

We seek to express $Dice$ in terms of $IoU$.

**Step 1 — Start from the Dice formula and substitute $TP = IoU \cdot (TP + FP + FN)$:**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \, IoU \cdot (TP + FP + FN)}{2 \, IoU \cdot (TP + FP + FN) + (FP + FN)}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $(TP + FP + FN)$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \, IoU}{2 \, IoU + \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + FP + FN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Note that $\dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + FP + FN} = 1 - IoU$** because $TP + FP + FN$ is the denominator of $IoU$, so $1 - IoU = \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + FP + FN}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \, IoU}{2 \, IoU + (1 - IoU)} = \frac{2 \, IoU}{1 + IoU}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Dice = \frac{2 \, IoU}{1 + IoU}\,} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,IoU = \frac{Dice}{2 - Dice}\,}
$$

This is **board-exam gold** — frequently asked as a "show the relationship" question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Record Style)

Suppose a binary segmentation model produces the following counts on the test set:

- $TP = 850$
- $FP = 50$
- $FN = 100$
- $TN = 9000$

**Step A — Compute Pixel Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{850 + 9000}{850 + 9000 + 50 + 100} = \frac{9850}{10000} = 0.985
$$

**Step B — Compute IoU.**

$$
IoU = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{850}{850 + 50 + 100} = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.850
$$

**Step C — Compute Dice.**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \, TP}{2 \, TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 850}{2 \times 850 + 50 + 100} = \frac{1700}{1850} \approx 0.919
$$

**Step D — Verify equivalence.**

$$
\text{Check: } \frac{2 \, IoU}{1 + IoU} = \frac{2 \times 0.850}{1 + 0.850} = \frac{1.700}{1.850} \approx 0.919 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Dice is always **≥ IoU** for the same prediction, which is why it appears more "generous" — a property the examiner may ask you to explain.

---

## 3.4 Multi-Class Extension (Per-Class mIoU)

For a $C$-class problem, IoU is computed **per class** and then averaged.

**Step 1 — For each class $c$, compute:**

$$
IoU_c = \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP_c + FN_c}
$$

**Step 2 — Macro-average across all $C$ classes:**

$$
mIoU = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} IoU_c
$$

**Example for 3 classes (cat, dog, background):**

| Class | TP | FP | FN | $IoU_c$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cat | 600 | 50 | 100 | $600/750 = 0.800$ |
| Dog | 400 | 80 | 120 | $400/600 = 0.667$ |
| Background | 8500 | 130 | 200 | $8500/8830 = 0.963$ |

$$
mIoU = \frac{0.800 + 0.667 + 0.963}{3} = \frac{2.430}{3} \approx 0.810
$$

---

## 3.5 Code-Level Implementation Notes (Lab Use)

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU lab report must include runnable code. Use the **soft Dice** form for stability:

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def dice_loss(pred, target, eps=1e-6):
    pred = torch.sigmoid(pred)            # (B, 1, H, W)
    target = target.float()                # (B, 1, H, W)
    intersection = (pred * target).sum(dim=(2, 3))
    union = pred.sum(dim=(2, 3)) + target.sum(dim=(2, 3))
    dice = (2 * intersection + eps) / (union + eps)
    return 1 - dice.mean()

class DiceBCELoss(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.bce = nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss()
    def forward(self, pred, target):
        return self.bce(pred, target) + dice_loss(pred, target)
```

For **per-class IoU** (used by PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes evaluators):

```python
def iou_per_class(pred, target, num_classes):
    ious = []
    pred = pred.view(-1)
    target = target.view(-1)
    for cls in range(num_classes):
        p_inds = (pred == cls)
        t_inds = (target == cls)
        intersection = (p_inds & t_inds).sum().float()
        union = (p_inds | t_inds).sum().float()
        if union == 0:
            ious.append(float('nan'))     # ignore absent class
        else:
            ious.append((intersection / union).item())
    return ious
```

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**Why is plain pixel accuracy a poor metric for medical image segmentation, and what is the preferred alternative?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Pixel accuracy counts *all* correctly classified pixels. In medical images, the **foreground (tumor/lesion)** may occupy less than 1% of pixels, while the **background** occupies 99%. A trivial model that predicts *all background* achieves **99% accuracy** while completely missing the tumor — this is useless.
- **(1 mark)** **IoU (Jaccard Index)** and **Dice Coefficient** focus *exclusively* on the foreground overlap and are insensitive to background dominance. They are therefore the **gold standard** metrics for medical segmentation.
- **(1 mark)** In clinical deployment, **Dice ≥ 0.85** is typically required; the equivalent IoU threshold is $\frac{0.85}{2 - 0.85} \approx 0.739$.
