topics.title: 2D SLAM with Lidar: Implement a 2D SLAM algorithm (e.g., Gmapping, Hector SLAM) using a simulated or real lidar sensor. Evaluate performance using standard metrics.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAL508 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** 2D SLAM with Lidar: Implement a 2D SLAM algorithm (e.g., Gmapping, Hector SLAM) using a simulated or real lidar sensor. Evaluate performance using standard metrics. # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **2D SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)** using a LiDAR sensor is a fundamental problem in mobile robotics wherein a robot must concurrently construct a two-dimensional map of an unknown environment while estimating its own pose (position and orientation) within that map, using range measurements obtained from a planar laser scanner (LiDAR). Mathematically, the 2D LiDAR SLAM problem is formulated as estimating the posterior probability distribution over the robot trajectory $x_{1:t} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_t\}$ and the map $m$ given a sequence of LiDAR scans $z_{1:t} = \{z_1, z_2, \dots, z_t\}$ and odometry/control inputs $u_{1:t}$: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ where each pose $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ is a 3-DoF (degrees of freedom) state in 2D space, $m$ is the occupancy grid map, and the inputs/outputs are produced by the LiDAR and wheel encoders/IMU. ### 1.1.1 GMapping (Grid-based FastSLAM) **GMapping** is a Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF) based 2D SLAM algorithm that uses an adaptive proposal distribution and selective resampling to efficiently build grid maps from laser range data and odometry. It was proposed by Grisetti, Stachniss, and Burgard (2005, 2007) and remains one of the most widely deployed open-source 2D SLAM solutions in the ROS ecosystem. ### 1.1.2 Hector SLAM **Hector SLAM** is a scan-matching based 2D SLAM system that estimates the robot's pose by aligning successive laser scans against an existing occupancy grid map using a Gauss-Newton gradient descent optimization. It does **not** require odometry, making it suitable for platforms with unreliable wheel encoders (e.g., aerial or handheld mapping). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to implement either GMapping or Hector SLAM (or both), execute it on a simulated environment (e.g., ROS + Gazebo + Stage) or a real differential-drive platform equipped with a 2D LiDAR (e.g., RPLIDAR A1/A2, YDLIDAR X4), and quantitatively evaluate the resulting map and trajectory using standard metrics. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **blindfolded inside an unfamiliar building** and you must (a) draw a floor plan of the corridors you walk through and (b) keep track of where you are on that floor plan — *simultaneously* and using *only a flashlight that measures how far walls are from you* (a LiDAR). - The **flashlight beam** that returns distance to walls is your **LiDAR scan** $z_t$. - The **odometry** (your internal sense of footsteps) is $u_t$, but it drifts. - The **floor plan you sketch** is the map $m$. - Your **mental estimate of "I am here on the sketch"** is the pose $x_t$. If your mental sketch is wrong, you'll misjudge distances. If your sense of position is wrong, your sketch will be distorted. SLAM solves the **chicken-and-egg** coupling by jointly refining both estimates. **GMapping** is like carrying *many* mental sketches (particles) and keeping only the most plausible ones. **Hector SLAM** is like carefully *sliding and rotating each new flashlight reading* until it perfectly overlaps with the previous sketch — no pedometer needed. > [!NOTE] > **Key Constants and Standard Metrics (highlighted in bold):** > - LiDAR scan rate: typically **5–40 Hz** (e.g., RPLIDAR A1: 5.5 Hz; A2: 10 Hz; VLP-16 (3D): 5–20 Hz). > - Range resolution: typically **±0.5 mm to ±3 cm** depending on sensor. > - **Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE)** — measures global consistency of estimated vs. ground-truth trajectory (units: **meters**). > - **Relative Pose Error (RPE)** — measures local drift over a fixed time/distance interval. > - **Mapping accuracy / F-score** — measures overlap between estimated and ground-truth occupied/free cells. > - **CPU/RAM load** and **real-time factor (RTF)** — practical runtime metrics. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 2D LiDAR SLAM Pipeline (Operational Breakdown) The end-to-end pipeline of a 2D LiDAR SLAM system can be decomposed into the following structured logical steps: 1. **Sensor Data Acquisition** - The 2D LiDAR emits laser pulses (typically 905 nm Class 1 infrared) and measures time-of-flight. - Produces a scan $z_t = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_N\}$ where $r_i$ is the range at bearing $\phi_i$. - Typical angular resolution: **0.25°–1°** (e.g., 360 beams at 1° for RPLIDAR A1). 2. **Motion Prediction (Odometry Update)** - Using wheel encoders or IMU integration, predict the prior pose: $x_t^- = f(x_{t-1}, u_t)$. - For a differential-drive robot: $x_t = x_{t-1} + \begin{pmatrix} v \cos(\theta) \Delta t \\ v \sin(\theta) \Delta t \\ \omega \Delta t \end{pmatrix}$. 3. **Scan Registration / Matching** - Align the current scan $z_t$ with the existing map $m_{t-1}$ to refine the pose estimate. - **GMapping** uses a *likelihood field* model with a RBPF proposal. - **Hector SLAM** uses Gauss-Newton minimization of point-to-map distances. 4. **Map Update** - Integrate the registered scan into the occupancy grid using the inverse sensor model. - For each cell $m_{i,j}$ hit by ray $r_k$, update the log-odds: $$l(m_{i,j}) = l(m_{i,j}) + l_{\text{occ}} - l_{\text{prior}}$$ 5. **Loop Closure (optional, advanced)** - Detect when the robot revisits a previously mapped area to correct accumulated drift. - GMapping relies on the particle filter's resampling; Hector SLAM's basic version lacks explicit loop closure. ## 2.2 Algorithm-Specific Theory ### 2.2.1 GMapping (RBPF) GMapping factorizes the SLAM posterior as: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ This **Rao-Blackwellization** moves map estimation *inside* the trajectory particles, allowing each particle to maintain its own small map. The proposal distribution $x_t^{[i]}$ is sampled from a Gaussian fit around the most likely scan-matching peak, reducing particle depletion. ### 2.2.2 Hector SLAM Hector SLAM minimizes a non-linear least-squares objective that aligns the current scan endpoints with the nearest occupied cells in the map: $$\xi^* = \arg\min_{\xi} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i(\xi)) \right]^2$$ where $\xi = (x, y, \theta)^T$ is the pose transformation, $S_i(\xi)$ is the world coordinate of the $i$-th scan endpoint after transformation, and $M(\cdot)$ is the bilinear-interpolated map value. The gradient is computed analytically and the update uses Gauss-Newton iteration. > [!NOTE] > Hector SLAM requires a sufficiently high LiDAR scan rate (≥ 20 Hz) and accurate scan-to-scan motion compensation, since it relies entirely on laser data without odometry. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formula Reference Table** — memorize these for lab viva and exam derivations. | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** | |---|---|---| | Robot pose in 2D | $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$ | 3-DoF state vector, units: m, m, rad | | Differential-drive motion | $x_{t+1} = x_t + v\Delta t \cos\theta$, $y_{t+1} = y_t + v\Delta t \sin\theta$, $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \omega\Delta t$ | $v$: linear velocity (m/s), $\omega$: angular velocity (rad/s) | | LiDAR endpoint in world frame | $S_i = (x + r_i \cos(\phi_i + \theta),\; y + r_i \sin(\phi_i + \theta))$ | Endpoint of beam $i$ in map coordinates | | Inverse sensor model (log-odds) | $l(m) = l(m) + l_{\text{occ}} - l_{\text{free}}$ | Log-odds occupancy update | | Occupancy probability | $p(m) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l(m))}$ | Conversion log-odds → probability | | Hector scan-match objective | $\xi^* = \arg\min_\xi \sum_i [1 - M(S_i(\xi))]^2$ | Gauss-Newton minimization | | Hector gradient | $\nabla_{\xi} = \sum_i \left[1 - M(S_i)\right] \cdot \nabla M(S_i) \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi}$ | Analytical gradient | | Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) | $\text{ATE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \vert \text{trans}(x_t^{\text{est}}) - \text{trans}(x_t^{\text{gt}}) \vert^2}$ | Units: m (meters) | | Relative Pose Error (RPE) | $\text{RPE}_\Delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-\Delta} \sum_{t=1}^{N-\Delta} \vert x_t^{-1} x_{t+\Delta} - \hat{x}_t^{-1} \hat{x}_{t+\Delta} \vert^2}$ | Units: m or rad per $\Delta$ steps | | Mapping F-score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$, $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $P$: precision, $R$: recall; dimensionless | | Hausdorff distance | $d_H(A,B) = \max\left\{\sup_{a\in A}\inf_{b\in B} d(a,b),\; \sup_{b\in B}\inf_{a\in A} d(b,a)\right\}$ | Map dissimilarity, units: m | | Real-time factor (RTF) | $\text{RTF} = \frac{T_{\text{processing}}}{T_{\text{wallclock}}}$ | RTF $\leq 1$ means real-time | | Particle weight update | $w_t^{[i]} \propto w_{t-1}^{[i]} \cdot p(z_t \mid x_t^{[i]}, m_{t-1}^{[i]})$ | RBPF importance weight | > [!WARNING] > The vertical pipe `\vert` is used above **only inside math mode** (`$...$`) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do not type raw `|` inside table cells. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) use 2D SLAM for navigation. - **Service robots** (vacuum cleaners like Roomba, delivery robots) rely on low-cost 2D LiDAR SLAM. - **Autonomous forklifts and AGVs** in industrial plants deploy GMapping/Hector variants. - **Disaster response robots** in unstructured environments use Hector SLAM due to odometry degradation on rubble. - **Self-driving cars** use 2D SLAM as a fallback for parking-lot localization where GPS is denied. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Hector SLAM Scan-Match Gradient We will derive the Gauss-Newton update step used by Hector SLAM. The objective is: $$ \mathcal{L}(\xi) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i(\xi)) \right]^2 $$ **Step 1 — Define the transformed scan endpoint $S_i(\xi)$** The $i$-th laser endpoint in the world frame, after applying a rigid transformation $\xi = (t_x, t_y, \psi)^T$, is: $$ S_i(\xi) = \begin{pmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{pmatrix} + R(\psi) \begin{pmatrix} s_{i,x} \\ s_{i,y} \end{pmatrix} $$ where $R(\psi) = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi \end{pmatrix}$ and $(s_{i,x}, s_{i,y})$ is the endpoint in the sensor frame. **Step 2 — Compute the gradient with respect to $\xi$** Using the chain rule: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i) \right] \cdot \nabla M(S_i) \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the Jacobian $\frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi}$** $$ \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -s_{i,x}\sin\psi - s_{i,y}\cos\psi \\ 0 & 1 & \;\;s_{i,x}\cos\psi - s_{i,y}\sin\psi \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the map gradient $\nabla M$** Using bilinear interpolation of the occupancy grid $M$: $$ \nabla M = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial M}{\partial x} \\[4pt] \frac{\partial M}{\partial y} \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Build the Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation** $$ H \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \nabla M \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} \right)^T \left( \nabla M \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} \right) $$ **Step 6 — Update the pose estimate** $$ \Delta \xi = H^{-1} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i) \right] \left( \nabla M \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} \right)^T $$ $$ \xi_{k+1} = \xi_k + \Delta \xi $$ The iteration terminates when $\vert \Delta \xi \vert < \epsilon$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$). ## 3.2 Derivation: Occupancy Grid Log-Odds Update The occupancy grid stores the log-odds $l(m_{i,j}) = \log \frac{p(m_{i,j})}{1 - p(m_{i,j})}$. For each beam $k$ that traverses cell $(i,j)$ before terminating at the endpoint cell $(i^*, j^*)$: $$ l_t(m_{i,j}) = l_{t-1}(m_{i,j}) + \begin{cases} l_{\text{occ}} - l_{\text{prior}} & \text{if } (i,j) = (i^*, j^*) \text{ (endpoint)} \\ l_{\text{free}} - l_{\text{prior}} & \text{if } (i,j) \text{ is along the ray but not endpoint} \end{cases} $$ with $l_{\text{prior}} = \log \frac{p_0}{1 - p_0}$ for prior probability $p_0 = 0.5$ giving $l_{\text{prior}} = 0$. **Conversion to probability** for visualization: $$ p(m_{i,j}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp(l_t(m_{i,j}))} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) Given estimated trajectory $\{x_1^{\text{est}}, \dots, x_N^{\text{est}}\}$ and ground-truth $\{x_1^{\text{gt}}, \dots, x_N^{\text{gt}}\}$: **Step 1 — Align trajectories via Umeyama/Sim(3) transform** to remove global reference frame bias: $$ S, R, t = \text{Umeyama}(\{x_t^{\text{gt}}\}, \{x_t^{\text{est}}\}) $$ **Step 2 — Apply alignment to estimated trajectory:** $$ x_t^{\text{aligned}} = S \cdot R \cdot x_t^{\text{est}} + t $$ **Step 3 — Compute per-timestep translational error:** $$ e_t = \text{trans}(x_t^{\text{gt}}) - \text{trans}(x_t^{\text{aligned}}) $$ **Step 4 — Aggregate:** $$ \text{ATE}_{\text{RMSE}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \Vert e_t \Vert^2} $$ ## 3.4 Implementation: Reference ROS Launch for GMapping The following is the **canonical ROS launch snippet** for running GMapping on a mobile robot with a 2D LiDAR — a frequently examined item in KTU lab viva: ```xml ``` ## 3.5 Reference Python Evaluation Snippet (EVO-style ATE Computation) ```python import numpy as np def ate_rmse(traj_est, traj_gt): """ Compute Absolute Trajectory Error (RMSE) after Sim(3) alignment. traj_est, traj_gt : Nx3 arrays of [x, y, theta] """ assert traj_est.shape == traj_gt.shape # Centre both trajectories mu_est = traj_est[:, :2].mean(axis=0) mu_gt = traj_gt[:, :2].mean(axis=0) E = traj_est[:, :2] - mu_est G = traj_gt[:, :2] - mu_gt # Sim(3) Umeyama alignment H = E.T @ G U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(H) R = Vt.T @ U.T if np.linalg.det(R) < 0: Vt[-1] *= -1 R = Vt.T @ U.T var_E = (E ** 2).sum() / len(E) s = (S.sum() / var_E) if var_E > 0 else 1.0 t = mu_gt - s * R @ mu_est aligned = (s * (R @ traj_est[:, :2].T)).T + t errors = np.linalg.norm(traj_gt[:, :2] - aligned, axis=1) rmse = np.sqrt((errors ** 2).mean()) return rmse, aligned ``` ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison | **Criterion** | **GMapping** | **Hector SLAM** | |---|---|---| | Theoretical basis | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Gauss-Newton scan matching | | Odometry required? | Yes (uses odometry as proposal) | No (odometry-less capable) | | Scan rate requirement | Moderate (5–10 Hz sufficient) | High (≥ 20 Hz recommended) | | Loop closure | Implicit via particle resampling | Not in basic version | | Map output | Occupancy grid | Occupancy grid | | Computational cost | $O(N_{\text{particles}} \cdot N_{\text{beams}})$ | $O(N_{\text{iter}} \cdot N_{\text{beams}})$ | | Robustness to dynamic env. | Moderate | Low (assumes static world) | | ROS package | `gmapping` / `slam_gmapping` | `hector_mapping` | | Typical use case | Wheeled indoor robots | UAVs, handheld mapping | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End 2D LiDAR SLAM Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Robot Platform] --> B[2D LiDAR Sensor] A --> C[Wheel Encoders / IMU] B --> D[Scan Preprocessing
deskewing and range filter] C --> E[Odometry Prediction] D --> F[Scan Matching Module] E --> F F --> G[Pose Refinement] G --> H[Map Update
occupancy grid log odds] H --> I[Current Map Estimate] G --> J[Trajectory Estimate] I --> K[Loop Closure Detection] J --> K K --> L[Pose Graph Optimization] L --> G I --> M[Performance Evaluation
ATE RPE F score] J --> M ``` ## 4.2 GMapping Internal Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Odometry u_t] --> C[RBPF Proposal Distribution
Gaussian around scan match peak] B[LiDAR Scan z_t] --> D[Scan Matcher
likelihood field] D --> C C --> E[Particle Set x_1 to t] E --> F[Per Particle Map Update
occupancy grid] F --> G[Resampling Step
selective resampling] G --> H[Output Map and Trajectory] E --> I[Weight Computation w_t] I --> G ``` ## 4.3 Hector SLAM Internal Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[LiDAR Scan z_t] --> B[End Point Extraction
Cartesian points] B --> C[Map Lookup M of S_i] C --> D[Compute Gradient
nabla M times Jacobian] D --> E[Build Hessian H] E --> F[Compute Newton Step delta xi] F --> G{Pose update below threshold} G -- No --> H[Apply delta xi to pose estimate] H --> B G -- Yes --> I[Occupancy Grid Update] I --> J[Published Map and Pose] ``` ## 4.4 Evaluation Workflow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Recorded rosbag] --> B[Run SLAM Offline] B --> C[Estimated Trajectory] A --> D[Ground Truth from OptiTrack or GPS] D --> E[rpg trajectory evaluation tools evo] C --> E E --> F[Compute ATE RMSE] E --> G[Compute RPE RMSE] B --> H[Estimated Map] D --> I[Reference Map from CAD or测绘] H --> J[Map Comparison Tool] I --> J J --> K[F score IoU Hausdorff] F --> L[Performance Report] G --> L K --> L ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Q: Define the 2D SLAM problem mathematically. State the role of the Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter in GMapping.** **Model Answer:** The 2D SLAM problem seeks to estimate the joint posterior: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ where $x_{1:t}$ is the robot trajectory, $m$ is the map, $z_{1:t}$ are LiDAR scans, and $u_{1:t}$ are odometry inputs. GMapping uses a **Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter (RBPF)** that factorizes this posterior as: $$p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t}) = p(m \mid x_{1:t}, z_{1:t}) \cdot p(x_{1:t} \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$ Each particle maintains its own trajectory hypothesis and an associated occupancy grid map. The RBPF reduces sampling complexity by conditioning the map estimate on the trajectory, allowing the computationally heavy mapping step to be performed only on the surviving particles after resampling. *(3 marks: 1 for posterior, 1 for factorization, 1 for role of RBPF)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: List any three standard quantitative metrics used to evaluate a 2D SLAM system. State the units and one limitation of each.** **Model Answer:** | **Metric** | **Unit** | **Limitation** | |---|---|---| | Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) | meters (m) | Requires ground-truth trajectory; sensitive to global reference frame (needs Sim(3) alignment) | | Relative Pose Error (RPE) | m and rad per $\Delta$ | Choice of $\Delta$ is arbitrary; does not capture long-term drift | | Mapping F-score | dimensionless (0–1) | Requires reference map; depends on resolution of both maps | *(3 marks: 1 per metric, units, and limitation)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a) Derive the Gauss-Newton update rule used in Hector SLAM's scan-matching module. Clearly state the objective function, the gradient, and the Hessian approximation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The objective minimized by Hector SLAM is: $$ \mathcal{L}(\xi) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i(\xi)) \right]^2 $$ where $S_i(\xi) = \begin{pmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{pmatrix} + R(\psi) \begin{pmatrix} s_{i,x} \\ s_{i,y} \end{pmatrix}$ is the transformed scan endpoint and $M$ is the occupancy map. **Gradient (1st-order necessary condition):** $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i) \right] \cdot \nabla M(S_i) \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} $$ The Jacobian of the endpoint w.r.t. the pose is: $$ \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -s_{i,x}\sin\psi - s_{i,y}\cos\psi \\ 0 & 1 & \;\;s_{i,x}\cos\psi - s_{i,y}\sin\psi \end{pmatrix} $$ **Hessian approximation (Gauss-Newton):** $$ H \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} J_i^T J_i, \quad J_i = \nabla M(S_i) \cdot \frac{\partial S_i}{\partial \xi} $$ **Update step:** $$ \Delta \xi = H^{-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ 1 - M(S_i) \right] J_i^T $$ $$ \xi_{k+1} = \xi_k + \Delta \xi $$ **Convergence:** Iteration stops when $\Vert \Delta \xi \Vert < \epsilon$, typically $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$. *(7 marks: 1 objective, 2 gradient, 2 Hessian, 2 update rule)* --- **(b) Explain with a block diagram how Hector SLAM processes a 2D LiDAR scan. Discuss one advantage and one disadvantage of operating without odometry. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (described):** 1. **Scan acquisition** → raw range-bearing measurements $z_t = \{(r_i, \phi_i)\}$. 2. **Cartesian conversion** → $S_i = (r_i\cos\phi_i, r_i\sin\phi_i)$ in sensor frame. 3. **Pose initialization** → use last known pose as initial guess $\xi_0$. 4. **Iterative scan matching loop:** - Transform endpoints via $\xi_k$. - Look up map values $M(S_i)$ using bilinear interpolation. - Compute residuals $r_i = 1 - M(S_i)$. - Compute map gradient $\nabla M$ via Sobel or central differences. - Form Jacobian $J_i$ and accumulate $H$, $b$. - Solve $H \Delta \xi = b$ → update $\xi_{k+1} = \xi_k + \Delta \xi$. - Check convergence. 5. **Map update** → integrate the registered scan using log-odds. 6. **Publish** → map topic, pose topic. **Advantage of odometry-less operation:** Robust on platforms with unreliable wheel encoders (UAVs, legged robots, handheld devices), and unaffected by wheel slip or wheel-deformation artifacts. **Disadvantage:** Requires high LiDAR scan rate (≥ 20 Hz) and large field-of-view for accurate interpolation; degrades sharply in featureless corridors (geometric degeneracy); and does not perform explicit loop closure, leading to large drift in large environments. *(7 marks: 4 diagram flow, 2 advantage, 1 disadvantage — distribute appropriately)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Compare GMapping and Hector SLAM on the basis of: (i) theoretical foundation, (ii) requirement of odometry, (iii) scan-rate requirement, (iv) loop-closure handling, and (v) suitability for UAV mapping. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | **Criterion** | **GMapping** | **Hector SLAM** | |---|---|---| | (i) Theoretical foundation | Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter | Gauss-Newton scan matching | | (ii) Odometry requirement | Strictly required as proposal prior | Not required | | (iii) Scan-rate requirement | Moderate (5–10 Hz acceptable) | High (≥ 20 Hz recommended) | | (iv) Loop closure | Implicit via particle resampling | Not available in basic implementation | | (v) Suitability for UAV | Poor (no wheel odometry) | Excellent (uses only laser) | *Verdict:* **Hector SLAM is more suitable for UAVs** because UAVs lack reliable wheel odometry and carry a fast-spinning 2D LiDAR. **GMapping is preferred for wheeled ground robots** with reliable encoders and moderate LiDAR rates. *(7 marks: 1 per criterion + 1 verdict)* --- **(b) Describe the standard evaluation protocol for a 2D SLAM experiment. Include the formulas for ATE (RMSE) and RPE, and explain when each metric is most informative. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Evaluation protocol:** 1. **Data collection:** Record a rosbag containing `/scan`, `/odom`, and (if available) `/ground_truth` from a motion-capture system or RTK-GPS. 2. **Run SLAM offline** using the recorded bag. 3. **Export trajectories** in TUM format (`timestamp tx ty tz qx qy qz qw`). 4. **Compute ATE** after Umeyama alignment. 5. **Compute RPE** for varying $\Delta$ (e.g., 1, 10, 50, 100 frames). 6. **Map comparison:** rasterize the estimated and reference maps, compute IoU, F-score, Hausdorff distance. 7. **Runtime metrics:** record CPU%, RAM, RTF. **ATE (RMSE) formula:** $$ \text{ATE}_{\text{RMSE}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \Vert \text{trans}(x_t^{\text{gt}}) - S \cdot R \cdot \text{trans}(x_t^{\text{est}}) - t \Vert^2} $$ **RPE formula:** $$ \text{RPE}_\Delta = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-\Delta} \sum_{t=1}^{N-\Delta} \Vert (x_t^{-1} x_{t+\Delta}) - (\hat{x}_t^{-1} \hat{x}_{t+\Delta}) \Vert^2} $$ **When informative:** ATE captures *global* consistency and is most informative for evaluating loop-closure performance; RPE captures *local* drift and is informative for evaluating odometry/IMU integration quality over short horizons. Use **both** for a complete assessment. *(7 marks: 2 protocol, 2 ATE, 2 RPE, 1 interpretation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students **forget the Sim(3) alignment** in ATE, leading to artificially inflated errors due to reference-frame mismatch. Always perform Umeyama alignment first. > - When comparing GMapping vs Hector SLAM, students often **state "Hector is faster"** without quantifying. Use CPU%/RTF as evidence. > - For the Gauss-Newton derivation, students frequently **omit the Jacobian of the rotation matrix**, losing 2 marks. Explicitly write $\frac{\partial R(\psi)}{\partial \psi}$. > - The log-odds update sign convention is frequently **reversed** (using `+` where `-` is needed). Re-derive carefully. > - Don't confuse **mapping F-score** (occupancy-grid based) with **detection F-score** (object-based). ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — memorize before the exam:** - 2D SLAM = **simultaneous estimation of robot pose and map** from LiDAR + odometry. - Formal posterior: $p(x_{1:t}, m \mid z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$. - 3-DoF pose in 2D: $x_t = (x, y, \theta)^T$. - **GMapping** = RBPF, needs odometry, moderate scan rate, implicit loop closure. - **Hector SLAM** = Gauss-Newton scan matching, no odometry, requires high scan rate (≥ 20 Hz). - **Hector objective:** $\xi^* = \arg\min_\xi \sum_i [1 - M(S_i(\xi))]^2$. - **Occupancy grid** uses **log-odds** update: $l(m) \leftarrow l(m) + l_{\text{occ/free}} - l_{\text{prior}}$. - **ATE measures** global trajectory consistency (after alignment); unit = **meters**. - **RPE measures** local drift over a time step $\Delta$; unit = **m or rad per $\Delta$**. - **F-score** for maps: $F_1 = 2PR/(P+R)$; **IoU** for occupancy overlap. - **Real-time factor (RTF)** must be ≤ 1 for real-time operation. - LiDAR typical specs: **905 nm** wavelength, **5–40 Hz** scan rate, **±2–3 cm** accuracy. - Common ROS packages: `slam_gmapping`, `hector_mapping`, `slam_toolbox`, `cartographer`. - Tools for evaluation: `evo` (Python), `rpg_trajectory_evaluation`, `map_evaluator`. - Typical pipeline: Scan → deskew → match → pose update → map update → loop close (optional) → evaluate. - For KTU lab record, include: rosbag file, launch files, parameter list, ATE/RPE tables, qualitative map screenshots, and runtime metrics.

topics.title: Visual SLAM: Implement a visual SLAM algorithm (e.g., ORB-SLAM, DSO) using a simulated or real camera. Evaluate performance using standard metrics.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAL508 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Visual SLAM: Implement a visual SLAM algorithm (e.g., ORB-SLAM, DSO) using a simulated or real camera. Evaluate performance using standard metrics. ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (Visual SLAM)** is a computational process by which a robot or autonomous agent, equipped with one or more cameras, incrementally constructs a map of an unknown environment while simultaneously estimating its own 6-DoF (Degrees-of-Freedom) pose (3D position + 3D orientation) within that environment, using image streams as the sole or primary exteroceptive input. A **Visual Odometry (VO)** system recovers ego-motion from sequential camera frames, while a full **Visual SLAM** system extends VO with **loop closure** detection and **global map optimization** (graph / bundle adjustment) to correct accumulated drift over long trajectories. The two canonical implementations mandated by the KTU 2024 PCRAL508 syllabus are: - **ORB-SLAM** (Mur-Artal, Montiel, Tardós, 2015) — a **feature-based (indirect)** sparse SLAM system using ORB keypoints, DBoW2 bag-of-words place recognition, covisibility & essential graphs, and three parallel threads (Tracking, Local Mapping, Loop Closing). - **DSO** (Direct Sparse Odometry, Engel, Koltun, Cremers, 2017) — a **direct** sparse method that minimizes **photometric consistency error** across a sliding window, jointly optimizing camera intrinsics, depth, and camera motion without extracting explicit features. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Distinguishing Marks** > Feature-based methods (ORB-SLAM) use keypoint descriptors + geometric reprojection error; direct methods (DSO) use raw pixel intensities + photometric error. ORB-SLAM is robust under illumination changes due to descriptor invariance; DSO is more accurate on textured scenes and supports rolling-shutter & photometric calibration. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Blindfolded Tourist in a Museum"** > > Imagine you wake up blindfolded inside an unfamiliar museum. You are given a torch (camera) and can rotate to look around. > > 1. Each flash of the torch captures a 2D image of a few paintings (keypoints / features). You remember their relative positions from the previous flash. > 2. By comparing consecutive flashes, you estimate how you moved (**front-end odometry**). > 3. As you explore, you mentally draw a map of corridors and rooms (**mapping**). > 4. Eventually, you recognize a statue you saw 20 steps ago — you are back where you started! You now correct your entire mental map to make the loop close (**loop closure + global optimization**). > > ORB-SLAM is the tourist who sketches every painting with detailed labels (descriptors) and matches them by label. DSO is the tourist who remembers the exact pixel brightness patterns and matches by similarity of light. ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Weightage) | Metric | Full Name | Purpose | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **ATE** | Absolute Trajectory Error | Global accuracy of full trajectory vs. ground truth | **meters** | | **RPE** | Relative Pose Error | Local drift over fixed time/frame interval | **m / m** or **deg / m** | | **RMSE** | Root Mean Square Error | Standard error aggregation statistic | same as metric | | **FPS** | Frames Per Second | Real-time throughput | **Hz (frames·s⁻¹)** | | **Tracking Rate** | % Successfully Tracked Frames | Robustness indicator | **%** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Visual SLAM Front-End Pipeline (5 Operational Stages) 1. **Image Acquisition & Pre-processing** - Capture monocular / stereo / RGB-D frames at rate $f$ Hz. - Optional rectification (stereo baseline, lens distortion removal). - Convert to grayscale (typical for feature-based pipelines). 2. **Feature Extraction (Indirect) / Photometric Sampling (Direct)** - Indirect: detect **keypoints** (FAST corners in ORB) and compute **binary descriptors** (BRIEF-rBRIEF). - Direct: sample a sparse set of high-gradient pixels (DSO uses 2000 active points per frame by default). 3. **Data Association (Matching / Tracking)** - Indirect: descriptor matching via **Brute-Force Hamming** or **FLANN** on KD-trees of descriptors. - Direct: track points by **photometric residual minimization** (Gauss-Newton) across consecutive frames. - Establish **2D-2D**, **2D-3D**, or **3D-3D correspondences**. 4. **Motion Estimation (Geometric)** - 2D-2D: recover **Essential matrix** $E$ via **Five-Point Algorithm** (or Eight-Point), decompose to $R, t$. - 2D-3D: solve **Perspective-n-Point (PnP)** with **EPnP / P3P / RANSAC** to obtain $R, t$. - 3D-3D: **ICP** (Iterative Closest Point) for stereo / RGB-D. 5. **Local Map Optimization (Back-End)** - **Bundle Adjustment (BA)**: jointly refine camera poses and 3D map points by minimizing reprojection / photometric error. - **Sliding Window Filter** (DSO uses 7-frame window). - **Loop Closure** via DBoW2 place recognition → **Sim3 / Sim(3) pose-graph optimization**. ### 2.2 Why ORB-SLAM Works — The "Three Threads" Architecture - **Tracking Thread (Real-time, every frame)**: ORB extraction → initial pose from previous frame via motion-only BA → local map tracking → decide KeyFrame insertion. - **Local Mapping Thread (KeyFrame arrival)**: process new KeyFrame → update covisibility graph → local BA → cull redundant KeyFrames. - **Loop Closing Thread**: compute BoW vector for KeyFrame → query DBoW2 database → Sim3 alignment → essential graph correction → full BA. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to compare ORB-SLAM vs DSO, the examiner expects: (i) feature-based vs direct, (ii) descriptor matching vs photometric error, (iii) re-localization built-in vs weak in DSO, (iv) loop closure ORB-SLAM has, DSO pure odometry (no loop closure), (v) DSO intrinsically calibrates photometric parameters. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \vert t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ | **Pinhole Projection** — maps 3D world point $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ to 2D pixel $(u, v)$ via intrinsic $K$ and extrinsic $[R \mid t]$ | Camera model for both ORB-SLAM and DSO | | 2 | $$K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ | **Intrinsic Matrix** — focal lengths $f_x, f_y$ and principal point $c_x, c_y$ | Pre-calibrated via Zhang's method | | 3 | $$\mathbf{x}_{c} = R \, \mathbf{x}_{w} + t$$ | **Rigid Body Transform** — world-to-camera frame | Extrinsic parameters | | 4 | $$\hat{p}_2^T \, E \, \hat{p}_1 = 0$$ | **Epipolar Constraint** — Essential matrix $E = [t]_\times R$ | 2D-2D motion estimation | | 5 | $$E = U \, \text{diag}(1, 1, 0) \, V^T$$ | **Singular Value Decomposition** of $E$ → recover $R, t$ (4-fold ambiguity) | Five-Point / Eight-Point solver | | 6 | $$\min_{R, t, X_j} \sum_{i,j} \rho \left( \left\lVert \pi(R, t, X_j) - x_{i,j} \right\rVert^2 \right)$$ | **Bundle Adjustment Objective** — reprojection error minimized over all visible 3D points $X_j$ and keyframe poses | Global / local map refinement | | 7 | $$E_{photo}(I, \xi) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} \left\lVert I_r(p) - I_t(\pi(\xi, p, d_p)) \right\rVert_\gamma$$ | **DSO Photometric Error** — robust Huber norm $\left\lVert \cdot \right\rVert_\gamma$ over active point set $\mathcal{P}$ | DSO direct cost function | | 8 | $$\text{ATE}_{rmse} = \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lVert \text{trans}(Q_i^{-1} S P_i) \right\rVert^2 \right)^{1/2}$$ | **ATE RMSE** — after Sim(3) alignment $S$ of estimate $P$ to ground truth $Q$ | Global accuracy metric | | 9 | $$\text{RPE}_i = (Q_i^{-1} Q_{i+\Delta})^{-1} (P_i^{-1} P_{i+\Delta})$$ | **Relative Pose Error** between poses separated by $\Delta$ frames | Local drift metric | | 10 | $$\text{Tracking Rate} = \frac{N_{tracked}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$ | Percentage of frames where pose was successfully estimated | Robustness indicator | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles**: Visual SLAM provides backup localization when GPS is denied (tunnels, urban canyons). - **AR/VR Headsets** (Meta Quest, Apple Vision Pro): ORB-SLAM-derived pipelines anchor virtual content to physical environments. - **Domestic Robotics**: Vacuum cleaners (iRobot) and warehouse AMRs use SLAM for navigation. - **Drones**: Visual-inertial SLAM (VINS-Mono) is the de-facto standard for GPS-denied aerial mapping. - **Medical Endoscopy**: Capsule endoscopy SLAM reconstructs 3D anatomy of the GI tract from monocular video. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Pinhole Camera Projection Model We derive the mapping from a 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w)^T$ to its 2D image projection $\mathbf{x} = (u, v)^T$. **Step 1 — World-to-Camera Rigid Transformation** The world point is first transformed into the camera's reference frame via rotation $R \in SO(3)$ and translation $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$: $$ \mathbf{X}_c = R \, \mathbf{X}_w + t $$ **Step 2 — Homogeneous Formulation** Introduce a scale factor $s$ to capture the perspective division that occurs in pinhole imaging. The full homogeneous mapping is: $$ s \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x & 0 \\ 0 & f_y & c_y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \\ \mathbf{0} \mid 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Intrinsic Matrix Factorization** Since the world is a rigid 3D Euclidean space, the transform has 6 DoF encoded in $(R, t)$. The intrinsic matrix $K$ handles the 2D image plane geometry: $$ K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & s_\theta & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ For modern cameras the skew parameter $s_\theta \approx 0$, simplifying to the canonical $3 \times 3$ form. **Step 4 — Final Pixel Coordinates** Carrying out the matrix product and dividing by the third component (perspective division) gives: $$ u = \frac{f_x X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = \frac{f_y Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y $$ This is the **fundamental equation of pinhole imaging** and forms the geometric core of every Visual SLAM observation model. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) The ATE measures the global consistency of an estimated trajectory $P_{1:n}$ against ground truth $Q_{1:n}$. **Step 1 — Time Synchronization & Association** Match timestamps between $P_i$ and $Q_i$. Discard unmatched poses. **Step 2 — Sim(3) Alignment** To remove the global gauge freedom (scale, rotation, translation), align $P$ to $Q$ via the optimal similarity transform $S \in Sim(3)$ that minimizes: $$ S^* = \arg\min_{S \in Sim(3)} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lVert Q_i - S P_i \right\rVert^2 $$ This is solved in closed form via Umeyama (1991): $$ \bar{P} = \frac{1}{n} \sum P_i, \quad \bar{Q} = \frac{1}{n} \sum Q_i $$ $$ \Sigma_{PQ} = \frac{1}{n} \sum (Q_i - \bar{Q})(P_i - \bar{P})^T $$ SVD: $\Sigma_{PQ} = U S V^T$, then: $$ R^* = U \, \text{diag}(1, 1, \det(U V^T)) \, V^T $$ $$ s^* = \frac{\text{tr}(R^{*T} \Sigma_{PQ})}{\sum \left\lVert P_i - \bar{P} \right\rVert^2}, \quad t^* = \bar{Q} - s^* R^* \bar{P} $$ **Step 3 — Per-Pose Error** $$ E_i = Q_i^{-1} S^* P_i, \quad \text{trans}(E_i) \in \mathbb{R}^3 $$ **Step 4 — RMSE Aggregation** $$ \text{ATE}_{rmse} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lVert \text{trans}(E_i) \right\rVert^2 } $$ This is the **single scalar number** reported in 95% of SLAM evaluation tables. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ORB-SLAM vs DSO | Property | ORB-SLAM2 | DSO | |---|---|---| | **Paradigm** | Indirect (feature-based) | Direct (photometric) | | **Feature** | ORB (oriented FAST + rBRIEF) | High-gradient pixels (no descriptor) | | **Cost Function** | Geometric reprojection error | Photometric intensity error | | **Map Density** | Sparse (~100–1000 pts/KF) | Sparse (~2000 active pts/frame) | | **Loop Closure** | ✅ DBoW2 + essential graph | ❌ Pure odometry (no LC) | | **Re-localization** | ✅ Bag-of-words | ⚠️ Limited (initialization only) | | **Calibration** | Geometric (intrinsics only) | Geometric + **photometric** (vignette, exposure) | | **Rolling Shutter** | ❌ Assumes global shutter | ✅ Native support | | **Robustness to Motion Blur** | ✅ (descriptor invariance) | ⚠️ Low (breaks photometric) | | **Accuracy (EuRoC MH sequences)** | ATE ≈ 0.08 m | ATE ≈ 0.04 m (slightly better) | | **FPS (single thread)** | ~20–30 Hz | ~20–30 Hz | | **Public Code** | C++ (MIT/RA-L 2017) | C++ (CVPR 2017) | --- ### 3.4 Implementation Algorithm (Pseudocode Outline — Lab Reference) > [!NOTE] > The following high-level pseudocode summarizes the lab execution flow. For the KTU 2024 lab record, students are expected to implement this either in **C++ (OpenCV + g2o)** or **Python (OpenCV, g2o Python bindings, or simple Open3D-based prototype)**. Note: full Python ports of ORB-SLAM are not production-grade; the lab accepts a simplified tracker using OpenCV's `cv2.ORB` + `cv2.solvePnPRansac` for evaluation purposes. **Algorithm — Lab Visual SLAM Tracker (Simplified ORB-Style)** 1. **Initialize**: Load video / dataset, read camera intrinsics $K$, create ORB detector. 2. **For each frame $I_t$**: 1. Detect ORB keypoints and compute descriptors. 2. Match descriptors with previous frame using **Brute-Force Hamming**. 3. If first frame: store keypoints as initial 3D map (assume depth from stereo / RGB-D / known object). 4. Else: solve **PnP (EPnP + RANSAC)** to obtain $R_t, t_t$. 5. Update trajectory log $\{(R_i, t_i)\}$. 6. Visualize: draw current pose axis on frame, plot trajectory. 3. **Evaluation**: 1. Load ground-truth trajectory (EuRoC `/state_groundtruth_estimate0/pose/data`). 2. Time-align with estimates. 3. Compute ATE using `evo_ape` tool → report RMSE, mean, median. 4. Compute RPE for $\Delta = 10$ frames using `evo_rpe`. 4. **Output**: Quantitative table + trajectory overlay plot (PNG). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Visual SLAM — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB sensor["Camera Sensor
Monocular Stereo RGB-D"] pre["Pre-processing
Rectification Grayscale"] feat["Feature Extraction
ORB FAST rBRIEF"] match["Feature Matching
BFMatcher FLANN"] geom["Geometric Estimation
PnP Essential ICP"] vo["Visual Odometry
Frame to Frame R t"] kfselect["Keyframe Selection"] ba["Bundle Adjustment
Local Global"] loop["Loop Closure
DBoW2 Sim3"] map["Global Map
Sparse 3D Points"] pose["Trajectory
Pose Graph"] eval["Evaluation
ATE RPE RMSE"] sensor --> pre pre --> feat feat --> match match --> geom geom --> vo vo --> kfselect kfselect --> ba kfselect --> loop ba --> map loop --> pose ba --> pose pose --> eval map --> eval ``` ### 4.2 ORB-SLAM — Three-Thread Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TrackThread["Thread 1 Tracking Real time"] A1["Frame Input"] A2["ORB Extraction"] A3["Motion Only BA
solvePnP"] A4["Local Map Tracking"] A5["Decision
New Keyframe"] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 end subgraph LocalMapThread["Thread 2 Local Mapping"] B1["Process New Keyframe"] B2["Update Covisibility Graph"] B3["Local Bundle Adjustment"] B4["Local Keyframe Culling"] B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 end subgraph LoopThread["Thread 3 Loop Closing"] C1["Compute BoW Vector"] C2["Query DBoW2 Database"] C3["Sim3 Alignment"] C4["Essential Graph Optimization"] C5["Full BA"] C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 end A5 -- "New KF" --> B1 A5 -- "New KF" --> C1 B3 -. "Map Points" .-> A4 C5 -. "Corrected Poses" .-> A3 ``` ### 4.3 DSO — Direct Sparse Odometry Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB I1["Frame I 1"] I2["Frame I 2"] I3["Frame I 3"] S1["Active Point Sampling
High Gradient Pixels"] S2["Inverse Depth Initialization"] W["Sliding Window
7 Keyframes"] P["Photometric Error
Sum over active points"] GN["Gauss Newton Optimization
Camera Pose Inverse Depth Affine Brightness"] OUT["State Estimate
Poses Depths Photometric Params"] I1 --> S1 I2 --> S1 I3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> W W --> P P --> GN GN --> OUT GN -- Marginalize Oldest Frame --> W ``` ### 4.4 Evaluation Framework — evo Tool Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR GT["Ground Truth
TUM EuRoC KITTI format"] EST["Estimated Trajectory
TUM format from SLAM"] APE["evo ape
Absolute Pose Error"] RPE["evo rpe
Relative Pose Error"] RES["Results
RMSE Mean Median Std"] PLOT["Plots
Trajectory Error Time"] GT --> APE EST --> APE GT --> RPE EST --> RPE APE --> RES RPE --> RES APE --> PLOT RPE --> PLOT ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** Define Visual SLAM. Differentiate between Visual Odometry and full Visual SLAM with a suitable example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Visual SLAM** is the process of simultaneously estimating the 6-DoF pose of a camera and reconstructing a sparse/dense map of an unknown environment from a continuous image stream. **Visual Odometry (VO)** estimates only the incremental camera motion between consecutive frames (or short windows) and does **not** maintain a globally consistent map — drift accumulates without bound. **Full Visual SLAM** extends VO by adding **loop closure detection** (recognizing previously visited places) and **global optimization** (pose-graph / bundle adjustment), which corrects accumulated drift and yields a globally consistent map. **Example**: A drone navigating a 2 km path using a downward-looking camera. The first 1.9 km of VO would drift several meters; the loop closure upon return to the start point snaps the trajectory back to the ground-truth loop, giving globally consistent localization — this is SLAM, not just VO. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for VO vs SLAM distinction + 1 mark for example)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** List the three parallel threads of ORB-SLAM and state the primary function of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Tracking Thread** — runs every frame; extracts ORB features, matches with previous frame, estimates current pose via motion-only BA, and decides KeyFrame insertion. (1 mark) 2. **Local Mapping Thread** — processes new KeyFrames; updates the covisibility and spanning trees; runs local Bundle Adjustment over the covisible KeyFrames and map points; culls redundant KeyFrames. (1 mark) 3. **Loop Closing Thread** — computes DBoW2 Bag-of-Words vector for each KeyFrame, queries the database for place recognition, performs Sim(3) alignment, corrects the essential graph, and runs full BA. (1 mark) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 marks] Derive the pinhole camera projection model. Show how a 3D world point $\mathbf{X}_w$ maps to image coordinates $(u, v)$ via the intrinsic matrix $K$ and extrinsic transformation $(R, t)$. Clearly state the role of $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$. **(b)** [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the ORB-SLAM three-thread architecture. Compare its front-end (feature-based) approach with the direct approach of DSO in terms of cost function, robustness, and loop-closure capability. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Pinhole Projection [7 marks]** We begin with a 3D world point in homogeneous coordinates $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$. **Step 1 — Extrinsic transformation (1 mark):** The world point is rotated by $R$ and translated by $t$ into the camera frame: $$ \mathbf{X}_c = R \mathbf{X}_w + t $$ **Step 2 — Intrinsic projection (2 marks):** Using similar triangles of the pinhole geometry, the projection onto the normalized image plane is $(X_c/Z_c, Y_c/Z_c)$. The pixel coordinates are then obtained via the intrinsic matrix: $$ s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Full composition (2 marks):** Combining the two steps: $$ s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \mid t \end{bmatrix} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}_w $$ **Step 4 — Final pixel coordinates (2 marks):** Expanding and dividing by $Z_c$ (perspective division): $$ u = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y $$ - $f_x, f_y$: focal lengths in pixel units (x and y directions). - $c_x, c_y$: principal point (image center offset) in pixel units. **(b) ORB-SLAM vs DSO [7 marks]** **Block diagram (3 marks):** See Section 4.2 above. The three threads (Tracking, Local Mapping, Loop Closing) operate asynchronously, exchanging data via the map and the covisibility graph. **Comparison (4 marks — distribute equally):** | Aspect | ORB-SLAM | DSO | |---|---|---| | **Cost function** | Geometric reprojection error $\sum \lVert \pi(X) - x \rVert^2$ | Photometric intensity error $\sum \lVert I(p) - I'(\pi(p)) \rVert_\gamma$ | | **Robustness** | High under motion blur & illumination change (descriptor invariance) | Lower under blur; better in low-texture + low-motion scenarios | | **Loop closure** | Full DBoW2-based loop closing | None (pure odometry; LDSO adds limited LC) | | **Re-localization** | Strong (BoW) | Weak (only re-initialization) | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 marks] Define the Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) and Relative Pose Error (RPE) used in Visual SLAM evaluation. Derive the ATE-RMSE formula starting from the per-pose error $E_i = Q_i^{-1} S P_i$. **(b)** [7 marks] Describe the steps to implement and evaluate ORB-SLAM on the EuRoC MAV Dataset. List the standard sequence categories (easy, medium, difficult) and report representative ATE values. **Model Solution:** **(a) ATE and RPE Definitions and ATE-RMSE Derivation [7 marks]** **Definition of ATE (1.5 marks):** ATE is the root-mean-square of the translational differences between the estimated poses $P_i$ and the ground-truth poses $Q_i$ after a global similarity transform $S \in Sim(3)$ alignment, removing gauge freedom. **Definition of RPE (1.5 marks):** RPE measures the accuracy of local motion over a fixed time or frame interval $\Delta$, computed as the relative-pose difference between two estimates and the corresponding ground truth over the same interval. **ATE-RMSE Derivation (4 marks):** After time-synchronization of $n$ pose pairs, compute the centroid of both trajectories and the cross-covariance matrix: $$ \bar{P} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i, \quad \bar{Q} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i, \quad \Sigma_{PQ} = \frac{1}{n} \sum (Q_i - \bar{Q})(P_i - \bar{P})^T $$ SVD $\Sigma_{PQ} = U S V^T$ gives the optimal similarity transform parameters $R^*, s^*, t^*$ (Umeyama solution). The aligned estimate is $\hat{P}_i = s^* R^* P_i + t^*$. The per-pose error in body frame is: $$ E_i = Q_i^{-1} \hat{P}_i $$ The translational part is $\text{trans}(E_i) \in \mathbb{R}^3$, and the RMSE is: $$ \boxed{\text{ATE}_{rmse} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert \text{trans}(E_i) \rVert^2 }} $$ **(b) ORB-SLAM on EuRoC — Lab Implementation Steps [7 marks]** **Implementation Steps (4 marks):** 1. Download EuRoC MAV Dataset (e.g., `MH_01_easy`, `V2_03_difficult`) from ASL ETH Zürich. 2. Install ORB-SLAM2 dependencies (OpenCV 3/4, Eigen3, g2o, DBoW2, Pangolin). 3. Build ORB-SLAM2: `./build.sh`. 4. Run on a sequence: `./mono_euroc Vocabulary/ORBvoc.txt Examples/Monocular/EuRoC.yaml $DATASET_PATH/MH_01_easy Examples/Monocular/EuRoC_TimeStamps/MH01.txt`. 5. Save the estimated trajectory (TUM format) to `CameraTrajectory.txt`. 6. Run `evo_ape tum groundtruth.txt CameraTrajectory.txt -va --plot --plot_mode xyz` to compute ATE. **Sequence Categories and ATE Values (3 marks):** | Sequence | Category | ORB-SLAM2 ATE (m, RMSE) | |---|---|---| | `MH_01_easy` | Easy (Vicon room) | ≈ 0.035 | | `MH_03_medium` | Medium | ≈ 0.045 | | `MH_05_difficult` | Difficult (dynamic motion) | ≈ 0.090 | | `V1_03_difficult` | Difficult (visual challenge) | ≈ 0.089 | | `V2_03_difficult` | Difficult | ≈ 0.097 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting time alignment** before computing ATE — the timestamps of the estimate and ground truth must be matched first; evo does this automatically, but custom code often skips it. > 2. **Confusing ATE with RPE** — ATE is *global* (full trajectory alignment), RPE is *local* (interval-based). Mixing them loses 2–3 marks. > 3. **Omitting $K$ derivation steps** — when asked to "derive", merely quoting the formula loses marks; show all 4 derivation steps with units. > 4. **Reporting only RMSE** — examiners also expect mean, median, std, and a qualitative description. > 5. **Forgetting to specify monocular scale** — monocular SLAM produces trajectories up to an unknown scale; mention the Sim(3) alignment $S$ that resolves this. > 6. **Confusing loop closure with relocalization** — loop closure is online map correction; relocalization is recovery from tracking failure. > 7. **Skew parameter** — for the pinhole model, some cameras have $s_\theta \neq 0$; mention this for full credit. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Visual SLAM = Localize + Map simultaneously using cameras**; VO alone has unbounded drift. - ✅ **ORB-SLAM = indirect / feature-based**, uses ORB keypoints + DBoW2 loop closure, three threads. - ✅ **DSO = direct / photometric**, no features, sliding-window optimization, joint intrinsic + photometric calibration. - ✅ **Pinhole model**: $s\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = K [R \mid t] \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$; $K$ contains $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$ (and possibly skew $s_\theta$). - ✅ **2D-2D motion** via Essential matrix (5-point or 8-point algorithm); **2D-3D** via PnP (EPnP + RANSAC). - ✅ **Bundle Adjustment** minimizes reprojection error (geometric) or photometric error (direct) over all observed map points. - ✅ **ATE** = global accuracy metric, RMSE after Sim(3) alignment, requires ground truth. - ✅ **RPE** = local drift metric, computed over fixed interval $\Delta$, more informative when global loop is missing. - ✅ **EuRoC dataset** is the standard SLAM benchmark: `MH_*` (Machine Hall, Vicon), `V*_*` (Vicon Room 1/2); 11 sequences split easy/medium/difficult. - ✅ **Evaluation tools**: `evo` (Python, most common), `rpg_trajectory_evaluation` (TUM), `KITTI devkit` (auto-driving). - ✅ **Tracking Rate** = $N_{tracked}/N_{total} \times 100\%$ — robustness indicator for video sequences. - ✅ **ORB-SLAM2 ATE typical range on EuRoC**: 0.03 m (easy) to 0.10 m (difficult). - ✅ **DSO typical range**: 0.04 m (easy) — slightly more accurate on textured scenes but no loop closure. - ✅ Always **rectify images** before feature extraction to remove lens distortion $(k_1, k_2, k_3, p_1, p_2)$. - ✅ **Loop closure** = re-observing a previously mapped place; **relocalization** = recovering pose after tracking loss.

topics.title: Robot Arm Control using PID: Simulate the control of robot arm using PID controller.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL508 - ADVANCED ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE AND MACHINE LEARNING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Arm Control using PID: Simulate the control of robot arm using PID controller.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) Controller** is a classical, model-free, feedback-based control mechanism that computes a corrective control signal as a weighted linear combination of the **proportional**, **integral**, and **derivative** of the tracking **error** between a desired reference trajectory and the actual measured output of a dynamic system. In the context of a **robot arm**, PID control is employed to drive each joint actuator (motor) so that the joint angle (or end-effector position) converges rapidly, stably, and with minimal steady-state error to a user-defined target pose.

Mathematically, the continuous-time PID control law is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where the **error signal** is defined as the deviation between the **setpoint (reference)** and the **measured process variable (PV)**:

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

Here:
- $r(t)$ = **Reference / Desired joint angle** (in radians or degrees)
- $y(t)$ = **Measured current joint angle** from the encoder
- $u(t)$ = **Control effort** (motor voltage / torque, typically bounded)
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = **Tunable gain constants** (non-negative scalars)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Context (PCRAL508):** In this experiment, you will *simulate* a single-link (or two-link) robot arm in software (Python with NumPy/Matplotlib **or** MATLAB/Simulink) and apply a digital PID controller to drive the arm from an arbitrary initial angle to a desired target angle. The lab tests your ability to (a) discretize the continuous PID law, (b) tune the gains, and (c) analyze transient response metrics — **rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Headed Driver"

Imagine you are **learning to drive a car for the very first time**, and your instructor asks you to park exactly **1 metre** away from a wall.

| PID Term | Driving Analogy | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional (P)** | You press the accelerator **harder the further you are** from the wall. Far away → full throttle; close → gentle tap. | Magnitude of correction is **proportional to the present error**. |
| **Integral (I)** | You notice that even after releasing the accelerator, the car **crawls forward slowly** and never quite reaches the wall. So you **accumulate** this small residual error over time and apply a tiny extra push. | Eliminates **persistent steady-state error** caused by gravity, friction, or unmodeled bias. |
| **Derivative (D)** | As you approach the wall, you anticipate that you are **closing in too fast** and **ease off the brake** before you crash. | Damps the response, **reduces overshoot**, and improves stability. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Robot Arm Translation:** The "wall" is the **desired joint angle** of the robot arm. The "car" is the **actuator (DC/Stepper/Servo motor)** driving the link. Gravity pulling the arm downward, friction in the gear train, and external payloads act as **disturbances** that the PID must reject.

## 1.3 Why PID for a Robot Arm?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Justifications (frequently asked in KTU viva):**
> 1. **Simplicity & model-free design** — No need to derive the exact Lagrange–Euler dynamics of a multi-link manipulator; PID works reasonably well for *single-joint* and *decoupled* control.
> 2. **Computational efficiency** — Only three multiply-add operations per control cycle → easily implemented on microcontrollers (Arduino, STM32) and real-time OS.
> 3. **Robustness** — Handles model uncertainty, parameter drift, and bounded external disturbances.

## 1.4 Standard Transient Response Metrics (used in KTU marking)

These four numbers **must** be reported in your lab record for full credit:

- **Rise Time ($T_r$):** Time to go from 10% → 90% of the final value. *(Lower is faster, but too low ⇒ overshoot.)*
- **Settling Time ($T_s$):** Time to stay within ±2% (or ±5%) of the final value. *(Indicates practical speed.)*
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** Maximum peak above the setpoint, expressed as a % of setpoint.
- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** $\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$. *(Should be ≈ 0 for perfect tracking.)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the PID Control Law

The control output $u(t)$ is the sum of three independent, physically meaningful terms:

### 2.1.1 The Proportional Term (P)

$$u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)$$

- **Effect:** Produces a corrective action that is **proportional to the current error**.
- **Behavior:** Larger $K_p$ → faster response, stiffer tracking, but also **larger overshoot and possible oscillation**.
- **Limitation:** Cannot eliminate **steady-state error** for a system with a non-zero load (e.g., gravity on a horizontal arm).

### 2.1.2 The Integral Term (I)

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

- **Effect:** Accumulates **all past errors**. As long as any residual error exists, the integral grows and pushes the system harder.
- **Behavior:** Drives the **steady-state error to zero** (a property formally called *servo regulation*).
- **Risk:** **Integral windup** — if the actuator saturates (hits voltage/torque limits) and the error keeps growing, the integrator accumulates a huge value that causes massive overshoot when the system finally responds. (Anti-windup schemes like *clamping* or *back-calculation* are required in advanced implementations.)

### 2.1.3 The Derivative Term (D)

$$u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **Effect:** Acts on the **rate of change** of error. Predicts future error based on current trend.
- **Behavior:** Acts as a **brake** when the error is decreasing too fast; **damps oscillations** and reduces overshoot.
- **Limitation:** Amplifies **measurement noise** (since derivative of noise is large). In practice, a **low-pass filter** is applied to the derivative.

## 2.2 Robot Arm Mathematical Model (Single Link)

For a **single rigid link** rotating about a fixed pivot in a vertical plane, the dynamics (derived from Newton–Euler or Lagrangian mechanics) is:

$$J \ddot{\theta}(t) + b \dot{\theta}(t) + m g L \sin\theta(t) = \tau(t)$$

where:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $J$ | Moment of inertia of the link about the pivot | kg·m² |
| $b$ | Viscous friction coefficient at the joint | N·m·s/rad |
| $m$ | Mass of the link | kg |
| $L$ | Distance from pivot to center of mass | m |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**) | m/s² |
| $\theta(t)$ | Angular position of the joint | rad |
| $\dot{\theta}(t)$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| $\ddot{\theta}(t)$ | Angular acceleration | rad/s² |
| $\tau(t)$ | Applied torque from the motor (control input) | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Linearization Trick (commonly used in KTU problems):** For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, reducing the model to a **linear second-order ODE**:
>
> $$J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} + (mgL) \theta = \tau(t)$$
>
> This linear form lets you apply classical control theory (Laplace transforms, transfer functions, root locus).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below — it covers 80% of the derivations and numerical problems in KTU exams.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Tracking error | rad (or deg) |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Continuous PID law | V or N·m |
| 3 | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]\,\Delta t + K_d \dfrac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$ | Discrete PID (position form) | Used in digital code |
| 4 | $\Delta u[k] = K_p(e[k]-e[k-1]) + K_i e[k]\Delta t + K_d \dfrac{e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2]}{\Delta t}$ | Velocity (incremental) form | **Avoids integral windup** |
| 5 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{\Theta(s)}{\tau(s)} = \dfrac{1}{Js^2 + bs + mgL}$ | Open-loop plant transfer function | Linearized single link |
| 6 | $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller transfer function | Laplace domain |
| 7 | $T_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | Approx. rise time vs natural frequency | seconds |
| 8 | $M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\,\%$ | Percent overshoot vs damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% settling time | seconds |
| 10 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ | Steady-state error (Final Value Thm.) | rad |

> **Boundary Conditions & Practical Limits (frequently asked):**
> - All gains $K_p, K_i, K_d \geq 0$ for a *minimum-phase* stable plant.
> - Sampling time $\Delta t$ should be **at least 10× smaller** than the desired settling time (Nyquist rule of thumb).
> - Control output $u(t)$ must be **clamped** to actuator limits: $u_{min} \leq u(t) \leq u_{max}$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Else Is PID Used?

Beyond robot arms, PID controllers are the **silent workhorses** of modern industry:

- **Aerospace:** Autopilot pitch/yaw control of aircraft and quadcopter drones.
- **Process Control:** Temperature regulation in chemical reactors, flow control in pipelines.
- **Automotive:** Cruise control, anti-lock braking (ABS), electronic stability control.
- **Biomedical:** Infusion pump flow regulation, prosthetic limb joint control.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Image stabilization in cameras, drone gimbal control.

> [!NOTE]
> **More than 95% of all industrial control loops in the world are PID-based** (per a widely cited survey by the ISA — Instrumentation, Systems, and Automation Society). Mastering PID is therefore a foundational skill for *any* control engineer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Continuous to Discrete PID (the form used in code)

The microcontroller cannot integrate or differentiate continuously; it operates at discrete time steps $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ separated by a fixed sampling interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 1: Discrete approximation of the integral** (rectangular rule, *left-endpoint*):

$$\int_0^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2: Discrete approximation of the derivative** (first-order backward difference):

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the continuous PID law** to obtain the **positional discrete PID**:

$$\boxed{\,u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]\, \Delta t + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}\,}$$

This is the exact formula you will code in the lab simulation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of Single-Link Arm

**Step 1: Take the Laplace transform of the linearized arm dynamics** with zero initial conditions:

$$J s^2 \Theta(s) + b s \Theta(s) + mgL\,\Theta(s) = \tau(s)$$

**Step 2: Factor out $\Theta(s)$ and write the plant transfer function:**

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{\tau(s)} = \frac{1}{Js^2 + bs + mgL}$$

**Step 3: Form the unity negative feedback closed-loop transfer function** with the PID controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s)\, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\, G_p(s)}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$:

$$T(s) = \frac{(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)}{J s^3 + (b + K_d) s^2 + (mgL + K_p) s + K_i}$$

**Step 5: Stability check** — For a *third-order* denominator to yield a stable closed-loop, all coefficients must be positive **and** satisfy the Routh–Hurwitz criterion. For this cubic $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0$:

$$a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (b + K_d)(mgL + K_p) > J \cdot K_i$$

This inequality gives a **practical stability bound** for tuning.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transient Performance from Damping Ratio

For a *dominant second-order* approximation, the closed-loop characteristic equation can be matched to the canonical form:

$$s^2 + 2 \zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

Equating coefficients:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{mgL + K_p}{J}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{b + K_d}{2 \sqrt{J(mgL + K_p)}}$$

From these, the **percent overshoot** and **settling time** are:

$$M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}\,\%, \qquad T_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Gain Tuning on Response

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most important table for the KTU viva and the lab viva-voce. Memorize the trend direction.**

| Gain Change | Rise Time $T_r$ | Overshoot $M_p$ | Settling Time $T_s$ | Steady-State Error $e_{ss}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase $K_p$** | **Decrease** ⬇ | **Increase** ⬆ | Small change | **Decrease** ⬇ (but never zero with P-only) |
| **Increase $K_i$** | Small decrease ⬇ | **Increase** ⬆ | **Increase** ⬆ | **Drives to zero** ⬇ |
| **Increase $K_d$** | Small change | **Decrease** ⬇ | **Decrease** ⬇ | No effect |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Students often say *"increase $K_p$ forever makes the system better."* It does NOT — too-large $K_p$ leads to **oscillation, instability, and even actuator damage**. Always justify the gain choice with a transient metric.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A single-link robot arm has $J = 0.5$ kg·m², $b = 0.2$ N·m·s/rad, $m = 1.2$ kg, $L = 0.4$ m. Gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s². The desired angle is $\theta_{ref} = 60° = \pi/3$ rad. A PID controller with $K_p = 30$, $K_i = 5$, $K_d = 8$ is applied. Compute the natural frequency $\omega_n$, damping ratio $\zeta$, settling time $T_s$, and percent overshoot $M_p$.

**Step 1: Compute the linearized stiffness term $mgL$:**

$$mgL = 1.2 \times 9.81 \times 0.4 = 4.7088\;\text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\omega_n$:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{mgL + K_p}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.7088 + 30}{0.5}} = \sqrt{69.4176} \approx 8.332\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\zeta$:**

$$\zeta = \frac{b + K_d}{2\sqrt{J(mgL + K_p)}} = \frac{0.2 + 8}{2\sqrt{0.5 \times 34.7088}} = \frac{8.2}{2 \times 4.166} = \frac{8.2}{8.332} \approx 0.984$$

**Step 4: Compute $M_p$:**

$$M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi \times 0.984 / \sqrt{1 - 0.984^2}} = 100 \cdot e^{-3.092 / 0.178} = 100 \cdot e^{-17.37} \approx 0\,\%$$

**Step 5: Compute $T_s$:**

$$T_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.984 \times 8.332} \approx 0.488\;\text{s}$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **critically damped** ($\zeta \approx 1$) with negligible overshoot and sub-half-second settling — an acceptable tracking performance for a single-link arm.

## 3.6 Complete Working Python Simulation (Lab Code Skeleton)

> [!NOTE]
> **Since PCRAL508 is a Lab course, providing a runnable code skeleton is part of the expected deliverable. Use either Python (preferred) or MATLAB.**

```python
# ============================================================
# KTU PCRAL508 — Module Experiment 2
# Robot Arm Control using PID Controller
# Single-link arm simulation
# ============================================================
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# -------- 1. Plant (Single-link arm) parameters --------
J   = 0.5     # Moment of inertia [kg.m^2]
b   = 0.2     # Viscous friction [N.m.s/rad]
m   = 1.2     # Link mass [kg]
L   = 0.4     # Distance to center of mass [m]
g   = 9.81    # Gravity [m/s^2]

# -------- 2. PID Gains (tuned) --------
Kp = 30.0
Ki = 5.0
Kd = 8.0

# -------- 3. Simulation settings --------
dt       = 0.001            # Sampling time [s]
T_total  = 5.0              # Total simulation time [s]
N        = int(T_total/dt)  # Number of steps
theta_ref= np.pi/3          # Target angle = 60 deg [rad]

# -------- 4. Initialize state vectors --------
theta    = np.zeros(N)      # Position
omega    = np.zeros(N)      # Velocity
u_hist   = np.zeros(N)      # Control effort
e_hist   = np.zeros(N)      # Error history
e_int    = 0.0              # Integral accumulator
e_prev   = 0.0              # Previous error (for derivative)

# -------- 5. Main simulation loop --------
for k in range(1, N):
    # Error
    e = theta_ref - theta[k-1]
    # PID terms
    e_int += e * dt
    e_der  = (e - e_prev) / dt
    u = Kp*e + Ki*e_int + Kd*e_der
    # Saturate control effort (e.g., ±50 N.m motor limit)
    u = np.clip(u, -50.0, 50.0)
    # Plant dynamics:  J*theta_dd + b*theta_d + m*g*L*sin(theta) = u
    theta_dd = (u - b*omega[k-1] - m*g*L*np.sin(theta[k-1])) / J
    # Euler integration
    omega[k] = omega[k-1] + theta_dd * dt
    theta[k] = theta[k-1] + omega[k]   * dt
    # Save for plotting
    u_hist[k]  = u
    e_hist[k]  = e
    e_prev     = e

# -------- 6. Visualization --------
t = np.arange(N) * dt
plt.figure(figsize=(10,7))
plt.subplot(3,1,1)
plt.plot(t, np.degrees(theta), label='Arm angle')
plt.plot(t, np.degrees(np.ones(N)*theta_ref), 'r--', label='Setpoint')
plt.ylabel('Angle [deg]'); plt.legend(); plt.grid(True)
plt.title('Robot Arm PID Control — KTU PCRAL508')

plt.subplot(3,1,2)
plt.plot(t, u_hist, 'g'); plt.ylabel('Torque [N.m]'); plt.grid(True)

plt.subplot(3,1,3)
plt.plot(t, e_hist, 'm'); plt.ylabel('Error [rad]'); plt.xlabel('Time [s]'); plt.grid(True)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

**Expected Output Behavior:** The arm starts at $0°$ and rises monotonically (or with very small overshoot) to the $60°$ setpoint within ~0.5 s, then maintains zero error — the textbook signature of a well-tuned PID.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Closed-Loop PID Control of a Robot Arm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Signal r at t equals desired angle in rad] --> SUM1((Sum Node))
    Y[Measured Angle y at t from encoder] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> E[Error e at t equals r minus y]
    E --> P[Proportional Block Kp]
    E --> I[Integral Block Ki divided by s]
    E --> D[Derivative Block Kd times s]
    P --> SUM2((Sum Node))
    I --> SUM2
    D --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> U[Control Signal u at t is motor torque]
    U --> PLANT[Robot Arm Plant Gp of s equals 1 over Js squared plus bs plus mgL]
    PLANT --> Y
    PLANT --> FB[Feedback Path to Encoder]
    FB --> Y
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Lab Simulation Algorithm (Digital PID)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Simulation] --> B[Initialize plant state theta, omega, e_int, e_prev to zero]
    B --> C[Set Kp, Ki, Kd gains and dt sampling time]
    C --> D[Loop start for k from 1 to N]
    D --> E[Read measured angle theta k minus 1]
    E --> F[Compute error e equals theta_ref minus theta]
    F --> G[Update integral e_int plus equals e times dt]
    G --> H[Compute derivative e_der equals e minus e_prev divided by dt]
    H --> I[Compute control u equals Kp e plus Ki e_int plus Kd e_der]
    I --> J[Clamp u between u_min and u_max for actuator saturation]
    J --> K[Apply dynamics J theta_dd equals u minus b omega minus mgL sin theta]
    K --> L[Integrate omega and theta via Euler method]
    L --> M[Store u, theta, e into history arrays]
    M --> N[Update e_prev equals e]
    N --> O{Done with all steps}
    O -- No --> D
    O -- Yes --> P[Plot response: angle, torque, error versus time]
    P --> Q[Compute metrics: rise time, overshoot, settling time, steady-state error]
    Q --> R[End]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tuning Methodology (Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Set Ki equals 0 and Kd equals 0] --> S2[Step 2: Increase Kp until system oscillates with constant amplitude]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Record critical gain Kcr and oscillation period Pcr]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Ziegler Nichols formulas]
    S4 --> T1[Kp equals 0.6 times Kcr]
    S4 --> T2[Ki equals 2 times Kp divided by Pcr]
    S4 --> T3[Kd equals Kp times Pcr divided by 8]
    T1 --> OUT[Well-tuned PID gains for first trial]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
```
