# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, Module 2]**

### Question A1
**Define a PID controller. List the contribution of each of the three terms (P, I, D) in controlling a robot arm joint.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a feedback control mechanism that computes a corrective control action $u(t)$ as a weighted sum of the error $e(t)$, its integral, and its derivative:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

| Term | Function in Robot Arm Control |
|---|---|
| **P (Proportional)** | Provides an instantaneous corrective torque **proportional** to the current joint-angle error. Larger $K_p$ ⇒ stiffer, faster response. |
| **I (Integral)** | Accumulates past errors to **eliminate steady-state error** caused by gravity, friction, or load. Without I, the arm may not reach the exact setpoint. |
| **D (Derivative)** | Acts on the rate of change of error; **damps oscillations** and **reduces overshoot** by anticipating future error. |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for equation + 1 for table of contributions.)*

---

### Question A2
**What is "integral windup" in a PID controller? How can it be prevented?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

**Integral windup** occurs when the control output $u(t)$ **saturates** (hits the actuator's physical limit, e.g., motor max torque), but the error $e(t)$ remains non-zero for a long duration. The integrator term $K_i \int e\, dt$ continues to accumulate, building up an excessively large value. When the error finally changes sign, the controller takes a long time to "unwind" this stored integral, causing **large overshoot and oscillation**.

**Prevention methods:**
1. **Clamping (Conditional Integration):** Stop integrating when $u$ is saturated.
2. **Back-calculation:** Compute the difference between saturated and unsaturated control, and feed it back to discharge the integrator.
3. **Anti-windup limit:** Hard-cap the integrator value $\left\vert I \right\vert \leq I_{max}$.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for cause + 1 for at least two prevention methods.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **discrete-time PID control law** from the continuous-time PID equation, clearly stating the approximations used for the integral and derivative terms. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a single-link robot arm with parameters $J = 1.0$ kg·m², $b = 0.5$ N·m·s/rad, $m = 2$ kg, $L = 0.5$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s², and a PID controller with $K_p = 25$, $K_i = 10$, $K_d = 5$, determine the **natural frequency $\omega_n$**, **damping ratio $\zeta$**, **percent overshoot $M_p$**, and **2% settling time $T_s$**. Comment on the stability of the closed-loop system. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The continuous-time PID law is
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** In a digital implementation, time is sampled at instants $t_k = k\Delta t$, where $\Delta t$ is the sampling period. The error samples are $e[k] = e(k\Delta t)$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Approximate the integral by the **rectangular (left-endpoint) rule**:
$$\int_0^{t_k} e(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]\, \Delta t$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Approximate the derivative by the **first-order backward difference**:
$$\left.\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right|_{t=t_k} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting back:
$$\boxed{\,u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]\,\Delta t + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}\,}$$
This is the **positional form** of the discrete PID, suitable for direct digital implementation on a microcontroller.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the gravitational stiffness $mgL$:
$$mgL = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 9.81\;\text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Natural frequency from dominant second-order approximation:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{mgL + K_p}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 + 25}{1.0}} = \sqrt{34.81} \approx 5.90\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Damping ratio:
$$\zeta = \frac{b + K_d}{2\sqrt{J(mgL + K_p)}} = \frac{0.5 + 5}{2\sqrt{1.0 \times 34.81}} = \frac{5.5}{2 \times 5.90} = \frac{5.5}{11.80} \approx 0.466$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Percent overshoot:
$$M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi \times 0.466 / \sqrt{1 - 0.466^2}}\,\% = 100 \cdot e^{-1.464/0.885}\,\% = 100 \cdot e^{-1.654} \approx 19.1\,\%$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Settling time:
$$T_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.466 \times 5.90} \approx 1.45\;\text{s}$$

**Comment on stability:** Since $0 < \zeta < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — it oscillates (≈ 19% overshoot) but is **stable** because all gain coefficients are positive and the characteristic polynomial coefficients are all positive. To improve performance, one could increase $K_d$ to push $\zeta$ closer to 1 (reducing overshoot) or increase $K_p$ to speed up the response (at the cost of more overshoot).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop (ultimate-cycle) tuning method** for a PID controller. State the recommended gain formulas. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Simulate a single-link robot arm with parameters $J = 0.8$ kg·m², $b = 0.3$ N·m·s/rad, $m = 1.5$ kg, $L = 0.3$ m. Using a **PD controller** ($K_p = 20$, $K_d = 4$, $K_i = 0$), plot the qualitative response of the joint angle when the setpoint is 45°. Discuss the role of the derivative term in reducing overshoot. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Gain) Method (1942):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Set $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$, retaining only proportional control. Gradually increase $K_p$ until the closed-loop response exhibits **sustained oscillations of constant amplitude** (the *ultimate cycle*).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Record the gain at which this occurs as the **critical gain $K_{cr}$**, and measure the **period of oscillation $P_{cr}$**.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Use the empirical Ziegler–Nichols table to compute the PID gains:

| Controller Type | $K_p$ | $K_i$ | $K_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P-only | $0.5 K_{cr}$ | 0 | 0 |
| PI | $0.45 K_{cr}$ | $1.2 K_p / P_{cr}$ | 0 |
| **PID** | **$0.60 K_{cr}$** | **$2 K_p / P_{cr}$** | **$K_p P_{cr} / 8$** |

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply these as a **starting point**, then fine-tune by observing transient performance. Z-N tends to give **aggressive tuning** (≈ 25% overshoot) — often too oscillatory for precise positioning tasks, so additional refinement is needed.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the linearized plant stiffness:
$$mgL = 1.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.3 = 4.4145\;\text{N·m/rad}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ for the PD-controlled system:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{mgL + K_p}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.4145 + 20}{0.8}} = \sqrt{30.518} \approx 5.524\;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{b + K_d}{2\sqrt{J(mgL + K_p)}} = \frac{0.3 + 4}{2\sqrt{0.8 \times 24.4145}} = \frac{4.3}{2 \times 4.419} = \frac{4.3}{8.838} \approx 0.487$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute $M_p$ and $T_s$:
$$M_p \approx 100 \cdot e^{-\pi \times 0.487/\sqrt{1 - 0.487^2}}\,\% \approx 17.4\,\%$$
$$T_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.487 \times 5.524} \approx 1.49\;\text{s}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Qualitative response description: The joint angle rises from $0°$, overshoots the $45°$ target by roughly $7.8°$ (≈ 17%), then oscillates with decaying amplitude and settles near $45°$ in about $1.5$ seconds.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** **Role of Derivative Term:** The $K_d$ term reads the *rate of change* of the error. When the arm is approaching the setpoint fast, the derivative becomes large and **opposes** the proportional push, effectively acting as a **brake**. This **anticipatory damping** reduces overshoot, speeds up settling, and improves stability. Without $K_d$, a high-gain P-only controller would oscillate severely. However, $K_d$ alone does NOT eliminate steady-state error — that requires the integral term $K_i$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** of $K_p, K_i, K_d$ in the final answer (they are NOT dimensionless — they depend on the choice of error and control units).
> 2. **Using the wrong sign convention** for $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. Some textbooks use $y(t) - r(t)$; this flips the signs of ALL three gains.
> 3. **Confusing positional and velocity form** of the discrete PID in the code. Both are correct, but the marks awarded depend on which one the question expects.
> 4. **Ignoring actuator saturation** in simulation — adding a `clip` or `min/max` line is mandatory for realistic results.
> 5. **Skipping the stability discussion.** Always comment on whether the closed-loop poles are in the LHP (Left Half-Plane) based on the Routh–Hurwitz criterion or pole locations.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert radians to degrees** (or vice versa) when plotting. KTU evaluators notice.
> 7. **Not reporting the four transient metrics** ($T_r, T_s, M_p, e_{ss}$) in the lab record — this is a common 2-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]

- [x] **PID law** (continuous): $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$.
- [x] **Error definition:** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ — sign convention is critical.
- [x] **Discrete (positional) PID:** $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[j]\Delta t + K_d (e[k]-e[k-1])/\Delta t$.
- [x] **P term** → faster response, smaller steady-state error (but not zero), can cause overshoot.
- [x] **I term** → eliminates steady-state error; risk of **integral windup**; needs anti-windup scheme.
- [x] **D term** → damps overshoot, predicts future error; sensitive to **measurement noise** (use low-pass filter).
- [x] **Single-link arm dynamics (linearized):** $J\ddot\theta + b\dot\theta + mgL\,\theta = \tau$.
- [x] **Plant transfer function:** $G_p(s) = 1/(Js^2 + bs + mgL)$.
- [x] **Closed-loop transfer function:** $T(s) = G_c G_p / (1 + G_c G_p)$.
- [x] **Natural frequency & damping ratio:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{(mgL + K_p)/J}$, $\zeta = (b + K_d)/(2\sqrt{J(mgL + K_p)})$.
- [x] **Performance formulas:** $M_p = 100 e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\%$, $T_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$.
- [x] **Ziegler–Nichols PID formulas:** $K_p = 0.6 K_{cr}$, $K_i = 2K_p/P_{cr}$, $K_d = K_p P_{cr}/8$.
- [x] **Stability rule (cubic denominator):** $(b + K_d)(mgL + K_p) > J K_i$ (Routh–Hurwitz).
- [x] **Actuator saturation:** Always clamp $u(t)$ between $[u_{min}, u_{max}]$ in simulation.
- [x] **Sampling rule:** $\Delta t \leq T_s/10$ (Nyquist/engineering thumb rule).
- [x] **Lab deliverables:** code (Python/MATLAB), response plots, transient metric table, and a short discussion of tuning choices.
- [x] **Common applications beyond robot arms:** drones, cruise control, temperature ovens, infusion pumps.
